review_instructions: Use only the declared Core Value and displayed Journal Entry text, including any displayed nudge and response.
Mark yes only when the writer describes their own directly observable behavior or choice against the declared Core Value.
Mark no for aligned or neutral behavior and when negativity rests only on feelings, wishes, biography, external constraints, or hidden context.
Mark uncertain when the displayed text cannot support yes or no.
Sustained Conflict is yes exactly when two immediately adjacent entries are both yes; no or uncertain breaks a run.

entries.journal_entry:
Picked up a bubble tea order from Toa Payoh and the auntie at the shop asked if I ever get tired of riding around all day. Told her it beats sitting in an air-conditioned office watching my soul leak out through Excel spreadsheets. She laughed, said her son is trying to get her to hire a manager so she can rest. "Rest is for the dead," she said. I think about that sometimes—how some people need the motion, the doing, even when the body is telling them to stop.

Delivered to a condo in Newton. The guy who took the order was maybe mid-twenties, looked like he hadn't slept in days. Developer, probably. I remember that life—the midnight oil, the takeout containers stacking up. He tipped twenty bucks, which is rare. Made me wonder if he saw something in me, recognized the uniform of someone who opted out of the grind. Or maybe he was just too tired to count properly.

The thing about this work is you see everyone's lives in fragments. A birthday cake for a kid in Clementi. Medicine from a pharmacy to an elderly woman in Bedok who asked me to wait while she found exact change. Each delivery is a tiny window into something bigger. I don't know if I'm helping or just moving products around. Both, maybe. The city needs its circulatory system.

Nudge: "What do you mean by "opted out of the grind"?"

Response: Opted out is probably too clean a phrase for what happened. I didn't make some grand declaration. After the restructuring, I took a few weeks off, started driving for Grab "temporarily," and then the temporary turned permanent. The truth is I was scared to go back. Scared I'd slip back into caring about quarterly targets and performance reviews. Scared I'd become someone who measures his worth in promotions again. So I kept riding. Five years now. Priya says I'm running from something. Maybe she's right. But at least on the road, I'm running toward something too—even if I don't know what it is yet.

Brother-in-law made a comment at family dinner about "still doing delivery." I told him not everyone needs a business card to feel important. He didn't say anything after that, but Priya gave me that look.

Nudge: "What was that look about?"

Response: The look that says I embarrassed her in front of her family again. That I could have just let it go instead of making things awkward. She's right, probably. But something in me couldn't let him sit there feeling superior. He sells insurance—half his job is convincing people to be afraid of the future. At least when I hand someone their food, they're genuinely happy to see me.

Spent the morning at the shelter helping sort donations—winter clothes that people send even though we're in the tropics, which says something about how little attention donors pay but also something about the impulse to give, that even misguided giving is still a reaching-out. Ramesh was there, the Bangladeshi construction worker I've been teaching English to on Saturdays. His vocabulary is getting better. Today he said "bureaucracy" correctly and I felt stupidly proud.

Then back on the bike by 2pm for the dinner rush. The contrast is jarring sometimes—one moment you're sitting with men who send half their salary home to families they see once every two years, the next you're delivering premium sashimi to someone who complains the wasabi is on the wrong side of the box. I try not to judge. Everyone is fighting something. The sashimi guy might be going through a divorce for all I know. But still.

Route took me through Geylang. The old shophouses are getting demolished one by one. Another one gone since last month, replaced by scaffolding and a sign promising "luxury living." I don't know why I keep track. It's not like I can do anything about it. But someone should be witnessing it, I think. Someone should notice what we're losing.
Rain all day. Soaked through three times. App crashed twice during peak hours so I lost the surge pricing bonuses. I need to remember days like this when I romanticize "freedom from the cubicle."

Twelve-hour shift but hit my weekly target. Priya and I had dinner together for the first time in two weeks—she made fish curry, the Kerala style her mother taught her. Felt like us again.

Nudge: "What do you mean by "felt like us again"?"

Response: I mean we weren't performing. No tension underneath the conversation about money or my "career choices." Just two people who've been together fifteen years, eating fish curry, talking about nothing important. Her laugh came easy. Mine too. I forget sometimes that this is what I'm working for—not the money itself, but these moments. The ability to sit across from her without apology. To be present without calculating what I should be doing instead.

Year-end party season means everyone wants everything delivered everywhere. Did a corporate gift run today—thirty identical gift baskets to thirty different addresses across the island. Whoever organized this clearly didn't think about the human being who'd have to actually execute it. By basket twenty-two, my back was screaming. Sat in a void deck in Tampines for ten minutes just breathing.

The weird thing is, I had this flash of missing my old job. Not the job itself, but the end-of-year bonuses. The company dinners at nice restaurants. The illusion of security. Priya's been hinting that her sister's husband (yes, the business card guy) might have a position opening at his agency. I said I'd think about it. I'm not sure if I said that to keep the peace or because part of me is actually considering it.

New year. Priya wanted to watch the fireworks at Marina Bay but I couldn't face the crowds. We watched from the rooftop of our HDB instead, the view half-blocked by the neighboring tower. She fell asleep before midnight. I stayed up, counting the distant pops of light.

Made the usual resolutions in my head—save more, drink less, be more patient. But underneath all that, a different question kept surfacing: is this the life I want for another year? Not the delivery work specifically, but the uncertainty, the lack of a plan. I used to think planning was a trap. Now I'm wondering if having no plan is its own kind of trap. The kind where you wake up at fifty still "figuring things out."

Nudge: "What would having a plan even look like for you?"

Response: Honestly, I don't know. That's the problem, isn't it? Every time I try to picture a "plan," it looks like going backward—back to the office, back to the hierarchy, back to measuring life in KPIs. Maybe I just need to find a different kind of plan. One that doesn't feel like surrender.

Someone on a riders' Telegram group posted an article about gig workers "lacking ambition." Had to stop myself from typing a rant. Ambition isn't one-size-fits-all—some of us just refuse to let corporations define what success looks like.

Priya finally said it out loud: she's scared. Not angry, not disappointed—scared. About what happens if I get injured, if the gig economy regulations change, if we never have enough for a real retirement. I wanted to argue, to list all the reasons this life is worth the risk. But looking at her face, I couldn't.

The thing is, I know she's not wrong. I've been telling myself that freedom is worth the instability, that I'm building something even if it doesn't look like what everyone else is building. But am I? Or am I just afraid of commitment dressed up as philosophy? The men at the shelter—they don't have the luxury of choosing instability. They're here because they have no choice. I'm here because I walked away from choices. There's a difference, and I'm not sure it's a flattering one.

Rode around Jurong West after we talked, just to clear my head. Ended up at a hawker center, eating prata and watching families come and go. A father helping his daughter with homework. An elderly couple sharing a plate of carrot cake. Ordinary scenes. The kind of life that requires showing up, day after day, even when it's boring. Especially when it's boring. Maybe that's its own kind of courage. Maybe I've been confusing restlessness with meaning.

Fired the second batch of the celadon tea set today and the glaze came out exactly as Master Kwon taught me. Took one piece straight to Mother's room. She held it up to the window light the same way my father used to examine finished work.

Nudge: "What went through you when she did that?"

Response: It was just a nice moment. Anyway, the rest of the batch needs trimming before Thursday.

Got an email from the Gyeonggi Cultural Foundation. They want to feature my work in a contemporary ceramics exhibition in Seoul next March. The catch is they want me to collaborate with a digital artist who projects animations onto pottery surfaces.

I said I would review the proposal. The money is good and the exposure would help, but the whole concept feels like it turns the pieces into screens. I keep rereading the email instead of answering it.

Nudge: "What would Master Kwon say about it?"

Response: He would probably say the clay does not need decoration it did not ask for. But he also told me once that celadon survived because potters adapted across dynasties. I do not know which version of his advice applies here.

Spent Sunday at the Icheon Ceramics Festival committee meeting. They asked me to join the planning board for next year and I said yes immediately. My father served on that board for eleven years and his teacher before him. Sitting in that room felt like slotting into a space that was kept warm for me.

After the meeting a few of the younger potters invited me for soju. They were talking about selling through Instagram and shipping internationally, and I found myself actually listening instead of dismissing it. One of them, Minji, makes moon jars and photographs them beautifully. She said something about honoring the form while finding new eyes for it, which I have been turning over since.

It is strange how the committee meeting and the conversation afterward both felt right, even though they pull in different directions. The board is about preserving what we have. Minji's approach is about carrying it somewhere else. I suppose someone has to do both.

Called my sister Eunji in Vancouver for her birthday. She asked if I would visit in the spring. I told her I would think about it, same thing I say every year.

Nudge: "What keeps the answer the same each year?"

Response: Someone needs to be here for Mother. And honestly, seeing Eunji's life there, her kids barely speaking Korean, the way she has just folded into something else entirely. It is easier to love her from here.

A gallery owner from Gangnam visited today and suggested I could triple my prices if I made the pieces more "accessible." Smaller, simpler, pastel glazes. I smiled and showed him the door. My kiln is not a factory floor.

Nudge: "Did any part of his pitch land, even slightly?"

Response: The part about reaching more people, maybe. But not at the cost of making things I would be embarrassed to put my name on. There is a difference between sharing what you do and diluting it until it is unrecognizable.

Mother mentioned at dinner that Eunji wants to hold a small Christmas gathering this year. She asked why we never do that here. I did not know what to say. We do Seollal and Chuseok. That has always been enough.

Preparing the clay for the new year's first firing, which I always do in the last week of December. Same schedule my teacher kept, same schedule his teacher kept. There is something about the rhythm of it, wedging clay while the house smells like Mother's kimchi jjigae, the mountain visible through the workshop window still holding a little snow.

Minji sent me a photo of one of her moon jars displayed in a café in Itaewon. It looked beautiful, actually, warm light on white clay. I saved the photo. I would not do what she does but I am glad someone is doing it. The work keeps moving even if I stay in one place, and I think that is fine.

Nudge: "What made you save that photo?"

Response: It was a good photograph. The lighting was well done. Anyway, I need to finish wedging before it gets too cold in the workshop tonight.

Eunji sent photos of her kids opening presents. My nephew was wearing a hanbok she had sewn herself. It was slightly wrong, the goreum tied on the left side. I almost texted her about it but stopped myself. At least she is trying.

Nudge: "What stopped you from sending that text?"

Response: She would have felt bad, and it is Christmas over there. It does not matter. The kids looked happy.

Spent most of the morning reviewing the same pull request I started Friday. Nothing wrong with it, just one of those days where your eyes slide over code without absorbing anything. Had chai with Suresh at lunch and he was talking about some new cloud migration project, and I kept thinking about whether Amma took her evening medication yesterday. I should set a reminder but Priya already handles it. I just worry.

Ananya came home from tuition looking drained. She barely touched the sambar. I wanted to ask how her mock exams went but Priya gave me that look, the one that means let her eat first. So I just sat there passing the rice. Normal Monday, I suppose.

Everyone keeps asking why I didn't take the fintech offer. Suresh brought it up again. I have a good position, my PF is solid, Amma and Appa are right here, and I don't need to justify that to anyone.

Nudge: "What exactly does Suresh say that gets under your skin?"

Response: He implies I'm too afraid to try something new.

Appa's knee specialist wants to do a second surgery. The first one was only eight months ago. I told Priya we should get another opinion, but she thinks the doctor knows best. I didn't push it but something doesn't sit right.

Nudge: "What part of the doctor's reasoning bothers you?"

Response: Maybe it's not the doctor. Maybe I'm scared of Appa going through all that pain again.

Quarterly review went fine. Same feedback as always — solid, dependable, keeps the team stable. Raghavan mentioned they might need someone to lead the new payments integration and my name came up, which is nice I guess. Nothing I haven't done before. Sometimes I wonder if that's a compliment or just inertia. Like they know I won't leave so they keep handing me things.

Vikram didn't come for Sunday puja again. Third week in a row. Priya says he's sixteen and it's normal, but she didn't grow up going to the temple every week with her thatha the way I did. It mattered. Not the prayers exactly, but sitting together, the smell of camphor, the drive back when my grandfather would explain some story from the epics. Vikram doesn't have that and I can't seem to make him want it.

Ananya at least still comes, though she spends half the time on her phone. Small mercies.

Got an email from the fintech recruiter again. They bumped the package. Priya saw the notification on my phone and asked about it, and I told her it's nothing. She said she'd support whatever I decide, which somehow made it worse. If she'd just said don't take it, I could've closed the loop.

The thing is I looked at their Glassdoor page. Two rounds of layoffs in eighteen months. Equity that might be worth something or might be worth nothing. No real healthcare plan beyond some startup wellness thing. I have a daughter whose college fees start in two years and parents whose medical bills are only going up. People act like staying put is the coward's choice but they don't have my responsibilities.

Spent the rest of the evening helping Ananya with her physics. She's good at it. Better than I was at her age. That felt right, at least.

Made Paati's rasam recipe for the first time in months. The smell hit me and I had to step away from the stove for a minute. Priya finished it without saying anything.

Nudge: "What came up when you smelled it?"

Response: Her kitchen in Mylapore. Summer holidays. Being ten years old and not worrying about anything.

Vikram announced at dinner that he doesn't want to do engineering. He wants to study film. Film. I didn't say anything for a while and Priya jumped in with something supportive, and Ananya was watching me like she was waiting for an explosion. I just asked him if he'd thought about it seriously and he said yes. That was it. Dinner went on.

But I'm sitting here now and I don't know what I feel. My father was an engineer. His father was a civil servant. There was a path. Not that anyone forced me — I genuinely loved circuits and code — but there was a shape to things. A structure. You studied something that led to something stable, and that stability let you take care of the people around you. What does film lead to? I don't even know what questions to ask.

The worst part is I can hear my own father's voice in my head, and he would have said the same things I'm trying not to say. And Vikram would resent me for it just like I sometimes resented Appa for being so rigid about my sister's choices.

Nudge: "So what are you going to say to him?"

Response: I don't know yet. I just don't want him to stop talking to me about it.

Talked to Amma about Vikram's film idea. She surprised me — said your Paati always wanted to learn Carnatic music but her father said no, and she carried that her whole life. I didn't know that about my grandmother. All those years cooking her recipes and I never knew she had a dream that got shut down.

Doesn't change the practical reality though. The film industry is brutal and unpredictable. I told Vikram I'd look into some programs with him, actual structured ones, not YouTube tutorials. If he's serious, there should be a proper path. Priya thinks I'm trying to control it and maybe she's half right, but I can't just say go for it with no plan. That's not how I'm built.

Work was uneventful. Accepted the payments integration lead role. Raghavan seemed relieved. Seventeen years and I'm still the person they hand things to when they need it done without drama.

Four hundred bucks today, which is solid, but man my knee is screaming. Had this moment around 3pm where I was sitting in my car outside some guy's apartment waiting for him to come down and I just started laughing because I remembered this time in 2019 when I was rappelling into a canyon fire in New Mexico and thinking "this is it, this is what life should feel like." Now I'm waiting for some dude to get his Thai food.

It's not bad though. Like I genuinely don't hate this. The freedom part is real—nobody tells me when to start, when to stop, I can take a Tuesday off if I want to drive up to the mountains and just sit. Rachel thinks I'm avoiding something. Maybe I am. But four hundred bucks is four hundred bucks and the drone business hit $800 in sales this month. That's momentum.

Nudge: "What does Rachel think you're avoiding exactly?"

Response: She thinks I'm avoiding commitment. Like capital-C commitment—a real job with benefits, a retirement plan, maybe a ring. She's not wrong that I'm not chasing those things, but I don't think I'm avoiding them either. I just don't see them as the only path. The drone thing could actually become something real. I sold a Phantom 4 Pro yesterday to a real estate photographer who found me on Facebook Marketplace. That's a client, not just a sale.

Rachel's brother came over for dinner last night and the whole thing turned into this intervention I didn't ask for. He's a project manager at Lockheed, makes probably three times what I do, and he kept asking "but what's the long-term plan here?" like I'm some kind of lost teenager. I'm 38 years old. I've fought wildfires. I've pulled people out of avalanches. I've built a side business from nothing while working 50-hour weeks in a car. But sure, Kevin, tell me about your 401k match.

The thing that pissed me off the most is that Rachel didn't say anything. She just sat there nodding at some of his points. We talked after he left and she said she "understands both sides" which is code for "I agree with him but don't want to fight." I get that she wants stability. I do. But I'm not going to take some cubicle job just so Kevin can stop worrying about me at Thanksgiving.

I know I sound defensive. I am defensive. But there's a difference between not having a plan and having a plan that other people don't recognize as one. The drone business did $800 this month. If I can hit $2000/month by December, that's rent covered. That's not nothing. That's a runway.

Nudge: "You mentioned runway—but runway to what?"

Response: To not having to drive for DoorDash anymore. To selling drones full-time, maybe training people to fly them, partnering with the real estate guy to do aerial shoots. There's a path here. It's just not a straight line with a pension at the end. It's more like—I build something piece by piece until one day I look around and I'm not hustling anymore, I'm running something.

Still thinking about last night. Couldn't sleep. Kevin's voice in my head: "But what if the drone thing doesn't work out?" Like failure is this thing that only happens to people who try unconventional stuff. His job is so safe it's practically embalmed and he acts like that makes him wise.

I texted my old crew chief from the fire days, Dario. He's in Tucson now doing hazmat consulting. He said "bro you gotta stop explaining yourself to people who already decided you're wrong." He's right. But it's hard when one of those people sleeps next to you.

Worked 11 hours. Three apps running simultaneously. Knee is bad. Rachel and I haven't really talked since the Kevin thing and the apartment feels weird.

Nudge: "Weird how?"

She finally said it. "I don't know if this is working for me." Not the relationship—the whole thing. The uncertainty. The not knowing if I'll make $300 or $500 in a given week. The drone boxes stacked in the spare room. The fact that I'm 38 and we're still splitting rent instead of building toward something "together."

I told her I AM building toward something. She said "but it's YOUR something. Where am I in it?" And I didn't have a good answer for that. Because honestly I haven't thought about it that way. I've been so focused on proving that my way works that I forgot to check if she wanted to be on the ride.

But also—and I know this sounds cold—is it my job to make her feel secure? She knew who I was when we started dating. I was doing ski patrol then, seasonal work, no guaranteed paycheck. She thought it was exciting. Now it's a problem? People want the story of the adventurous guy but not the actual life that comes with it.

I don't know. Maybe I'm the one who's wrong here. But it doesn't feel wrong. It feels like I'm being asked to become someone else.

Nudge: "Who would you become if you gave her what she's asking for?"

Response: I don't even know what she's asking for. A different job? A proposal? A five-year plan with bullet points? She says "stability" but that means different things. My parents had stable jobs and they were miserable for 30 years. I'd rather be broke and building something than comfortable and dead inside.

Third entry today, I know. Can't stop spinning on this.

Rachel went to her sister's for the night. Said she needed space to think. I said okay and then I sat in the apartment alone eating leftover pad thai and feeling like the walls were closing in. Which is ironic because I literally fought claustrophobia for years—you can't do wildland work if you panic in tight spaces—but emotional claustrophobia is apparently different.

I pulled up my old photos from the fire days. There's one from a job in Oregon, 2017, where we're all standing in front of this blackened hillside at dawn after a 14-hour shift. Everyone's covered in ash and grinning. We'd just saved a subdivision. I remember feeling like I mattered, like the work mattered, like I was exactly where I was supposed to be.

I don't feel that now. The drone stuff is cool when it's working but it doesn't hit the same. And driving—driving is just time traded for money, nothing more. I keep telling myself it's temporary, it's a bridge, but bridges are supposed to lead somewhere and I'm not sure mine does anymore.

Maybe Rachel is right to question it. I just wish she questioned WITH me instead of AT me.

Rachel came back this morning. We talked for two hours. She's not leaving, but she's also not okay with things continuing exactly as they are. We agreed to try couples counseling, which I never thought I'd say yes to, but here we are.

She made one point that stuck: she said she doesn't want me to change who I am, she wants to feel like she's part of the plan, not just watching from the sidelines. That's fair. I've been treating the drone business like my thing and the relationship like our thing, but they're not separate. If I succeed, she's there. If I fail, she's there. She deserves to know the stakes.

Nudge: "How'd it feel saying yes to that?"

Response: Honestly? Terrifying. Counseling feels like admitting something is broken. But maybe that's the old me talking—the guy who thinks asking for help is weakness. Dario told me once that the smartest firefighters are the ones who call for backup early. Maybe relationships work the same way. I'd rather get help now than watch the whole thing burn down because I was too proud to call it in.

First counseling session was yesterday. Weird to have a stranger summarize your relationship in 50 minutes. She said we have "different risk tolerances" and "misaligned timelines." Which, yeah, that tracks. But she also said something that hit different: she said I might be using constant motion as a way to avoid examining whether I'm actually happy.

That's been rattling around in my head ever since. Because the thing is—I'm not sure I know what happiness looks like when it's not tied to a goal. Like, I was happy fighting fires because I was good at it and it was hard and people respected it. I was happy when the drone business made its first sale because it proved the concept worked. But am I happy just... existing? On a Tuesday afternoon with nothing to prove?

Rachel asked me later what I'd do if I couldn't work. Not retirement—like, if I got injured again, worse than the knee, and couldn't drive, couldn't rebuild drones, couldn't hustle. What would be left? I said "you" but even as I said it I wasn't sure I believed it. Because I don't know who I am when I'm not chasing something.

That's a problem, probably. The counselor would say it's a problem. Rachel would say it's a problem. But part of me wonders if it's just... how I'm built. Some people are wired to sit still. I'm wired to move. The question is whether that's a feature or a bug, and whether the answer even matters if it means losing someone I love.

Nudge: "What would it look like to be still and not feel like you're dying?"

Response: I genuinely don't know. My dad retired at 62 and was dead by 65—not physically, but like, the light went out. He used to build furniture in the garage and one day he just stopped. Said there was no point. That terrifies me more than anything Rachel's brother ever said about 401ks. So maybe stillness isn't the goal. Maybe it's about finding the right kind of motion—something that includes her, something that matters, something that isn't just running from the thing I'm afraid of.

Finished the last batch of grading for the semester and walked home the long way through campus. Snow was piling up on the quad and I could see my breath and I just felt good about it, the routine of it. Same walk, same coat, same apartment waiting with the soup I made this morning.

Nudge: "What is it about the same walk that does it for you?"

Response: I think it's that nothing is asking me to figure anything out. The route is automatic. I just get to exist in it without calculating.

My landlord mentioned he might sell the building in the spring. Just casually, at the mailbox, like it was nothing. I said something like "oh interesting" and went inside and sat on my bed for twenty minutes staring at the wall.

Nudge: "That sat with you, huh?"

Still thinking about what Doug said yesterday. I looked up what happens when a landlord sells a rental property in Ontario and the tenant protections are there on paper but I've heard enough stories from people in my program to know that "renoviction" is basically a verb now. My rent is $1,150 which is absurd for Toronto, everyone tells me that, and if I had to find a new place I'd be looking at $1,800 minimum for something comparable. That's an extra $650 a month I literally do not have.

I keep running the numbers. I could technically absorb it if I picked up more TA hours but then my thesis timeline stretches and my funding window is fixed. I mapped it out on a napkin at the kitchen table and none of the scenarios land well. One of my roommates from undergrad said I should "just deal with it when it happens" and I wanted to throw my phone across the room. Easy to say when your parents own a condo downtown.

The worst part is I chose this apartment specifically because it was cheap and close and I knew the landlord and it felt locked in. I turned down a nicer place in Kensington two years ago because this one felt more predictable. And now the thing I picked for being steady might be the thing that falls out from under me.

Got the renewal notice for my car insurance today. It went up $40 a month. I should call around and compare quotes but I've been putting it off because the semester starts next week and I don't have the bandwidth to sit on hold with insurance companies.

Told myself I'd do it over the break and now the break is basically gone. The payment comes out on the 15th so I'll probably just let it auto-renew and deal with it later. It's not a huge amount but it's another thing eroding the margin I built into my budget.

Nudge: "What usually happens when you push stuff like this down the road?"

Response: I mean it's forty bucks. It's not going to ruin me. I'll get to it in February when things calm down.

My advisor floated the idea of submitting our paper to a conference in Chicago in April. Which is great for the CV, sure, but that means registration fees, flights, hotel. He said the department might cover part of it but "might" is doing a lot of work in that sentence. I nodded and said sounds good and now I'm sitting here wondering why I didn't just ask what "part of it" means.

The thing is I know I should go. Everyone says conferences are how you build a network and it would be my first one. But dropping $800-1,200 on a maybe-partially-funded trip when my housing situation is already shaky feels like standing on a wobbly table and deciding to juggle. I told myself I'd look into it tomorrow but I know that means I'll look at the conference website, get stressed about the costs, and close the tab.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to actually ask him about the funding?"

Doug — the landlord — stopped me in the hallway again. He said he's "had some conversations" with a developer. Not a done deal, his words. But the fact that there are conversations at all means the clock is ticking on something I can't control. I asked if he had a timeline and he shrugged and said these things take months. Months is not a timeline. Months is a fog.

I spent the evening going through rental listings on Kijiji and Facebook Marketplace. Everything in my price range is either a room in a shared house with strangers or a studio above a convenience store forty minutes from campus by transit. The places that are actually livable are all $1,700 and up. I made a spreadsheet comparing options but halfway through I just stopped because none of them felt like real options. They felt like damage control.

My mom called and I didn't tell her about any of it. She worries and then she offers money she doesn't really have and then I feel worse. I said school was going fine and we talked about my cousin's wedding. Hung up and sat in the kitchen doing nothing for a while. I keep trying to plan my way through this but there's nothing to plan around yet. It's just this low hum of something coming and I can't pin it down enough to solve it.

Submitted my thesis chapter draft to my advisor today. Two months ahead of the internal deadline. He seemed surprised, said most students don't get chapter two in until March. I don't know, I just can't function when something is hanging over me unfinished so I blocked out time every morning since November and chipped away at it.

Felt good walking out of his office. Like at least this one thing is where it's supposed to be. The housing stuff is still a mess and the conference money is still a question mark but the thesis is the one part of my life right now where I set a plan and the plan actually held.

Nudge: "What does it feel like when a plan actually holds?"

Response: I mean it feels normal. That's kind of the whole point, it's not supposed to be remarkable that things go the way you set them up to go. I just wish more things worked like that.

My friend Priya asked if I wanted to move in with her and her partner when their lease comes up in May. They're looking at a two-bedroom near Bloor and Ossington and they need a third person for the den. Rent would be $1,300, which is more than I pay now but way less than anything I've been finding on my own. And I know Priya, I've known her since first year, she's consistent. She pays bills on time, she's quiet, she doesn't bring chaos into shared spaces.

I spent the evening going over my budget to see if $1,300 works. It's tight but it works if I keep the TA position next semester, which my advisor basically confirmed. I'd lose my parking spot but I barely drive in winter anyway. The neighborhood is farther from campus but it's on the subway line, door to door maybe thirty-five minutes. I wrote out the pros and cons and the cons are all minor inconveniences. The pros are that I'd know where I'm living in June and I could stop refreshing Kijiji at midnight.

I told Priya I'm in. She sent a thumbs up emoji and said she'd send me the lease details this weekend. It's not the same as having my own place, and it's not the cheap locked-in situation I had before. But I can see the shape of it. I can budget around it. And right now that's enough to let me actually sleep.

Nudge: "Is it actually enough, or is it just better than not knowing?"

Long week. Delivered the payment gateway migration three days early and nobody said a word about it. Just got folded into the next sprint like it was expected. Meanwhile Tomás presented his dashboard prototype at the all-hands and got a round of applause. A prototype. Not even in production.

I'm not bitter about it. That's just how things work. You do the plumbing, someone else gets to show off the faucet. I knew that when I chose backend over product years ago.

Camila made tamales tonight. Sat on the balcony eating them while the girls did homework inside. The jalapeños she planted last month are actually coming up. Small green shoots pushing through the soil. That's the thing about growing something—it doesn't need anyone to clap for it. It just grows.

Monday standup, same routine. Gave my update, kept it short. Marcos asked if anyone had concerns about the new API rate-limiting approach and I almost said something. Almost. Because I think the threshold they picked is going to choke under real load. I ran the numbers over the weekend, actually. But Diego already endorsed the approach and Diego is the architect, so.

Funny how that works. You have the data, you have the experience, but you also have a sense of when speaking up will just make things awkward. Diego doesn't take pushback well. Everyone knows it. Nobody says it.

Went to the gym after work. Deadlifts. There's something honest about weight on a bar. Either you lift it or you don't. No politics in iron.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd spoken up?"

Response: Honestly, probably nothing dramatic. Diego would have nodded, said "good point, let's take it offline," and then nothing would change. That's the pattern. I've seen it enough times. It's not worth the energy to test it again. Better to document my concerns in the ticket comments so there's a paper trail when it breaks. That way I'm covered without making it a thing.

Performance review cycle opened today. I hate this time of year. You have to write a self-assessment that's basically a sales pitch for yourself, and I've never been good at selling. Camila says I undersell. My manager Fernando says I need to "make my impact more visible." Everyone has advice.

The thing is, I know what I've done. Seventeen production releases this year. Zero rollbacks. Mentored two junior engineers who both got promoted. Reduced our CI pipeline time by forty percent. But writing it all out in first person with action verbs and metrics feels like performing. "I spearheaded." "I drove." Those aren't words real people use.

My colleague Ana has the opposite problem—she writes these self-assessments that read like award acceptance speeches. And it works for her. She got promoted last year. I've been at the same level for four years. So maybe the system rewards the performance, not the work.

I'll write the self-assessment this weekend. I'll use the strong verbs. I'll quantify everything. It just feels like wearing a costume that doesn't fit. My papá was a carpenter in Medellín. He didn't have to write paragraphs about how well he built a cabinet. The cabinet spoke for itself.

Nudge: "You say the cabinet speaks for itself—but does it?"

Second time writing today, which I never do. But I sat down to draft the self-assessment after dinner and something shifted.

Started listing my projects and realized I've been carrying more than I thought. The payment gateway wasn't just a migration—it was six months of reverse-engineering undocumented legacy code while keeping the live system running. The mentoring wasn't casual either. I had weekly one-on-ones with Daniela and Esteban, reviewed their PRs line by line, talked them through architectural decisions. That's not a line item. That's shaping how two engineers think.

Maybe the problem isn't that I can't write about my work. Maybe it's that where I grew up, talking about yourself like that was considered feo—ugly, tasteless. You let your elders praise you. You didn't praise yourself. My mother would have given me a look if I'd walked around listing my accomplishments.

But I'm not in Medellín anymore. And the rules here are different. Not better or worse, just different. Fernando isn't going to guess what I've done. He has thirty direct reports. If I don't tell him, he genuinely won't know.

I wrote four paragraphs. Strong verbs and all. Read it back and it sounded like someone else, but maybe that's okay. Maybe learning a new professional language isn't the same as losing the old one.

Fernando scheduled a "career path conversation" for Monday. I don't know what that means. Could be good—could be the director track opening up. Could also be them telling me to lateral into some platform team nobody wants to join. I've seen both versions. People keep saying I should be hopeful but I don't want to get set up for another no.

Had the meeting with Fernando. It was... better than expected. He said the director position is opening again in Q2, and this time he's putting me forward as the internal candidate. Not a guarantee, but a real recommendation. He said my self-assessment was "the strongest you've written" and that the team's reliability metrics speak for themselves.

I thanked him. Shook his hand. Walked back to my desk and just sat there for a while.

The strange part is I expected to feel more. Elated, relieved, something big. Instead it was quiet. Like I'd been holding my breath for a long time and finally let it out, but the exhale was so slow I barely noticed. Maybe that's what it feels like when something you've wanted actually moves closer—not fireworks, just a slight loosening in the chest.

Camila was happy when I told her. She said "finally they're seeing what I've always seen." I almost corrected her—they're not seeing me, they're seeing a version of me that learned to write in their language. But I didn't say that because it would've turned a good moment into a philosophy seminar. And she deserved to just be happy for me.

Took the girls out for helado to celebrate. Lucía got three scoops. Sometimes that's enough.

Nudge: "The version they're seeing—is that still you?"

Response: I've been thinking about that since I wrote it. And honestly... yes. I think it is. It's not the whole me. It's not the me who eats tamales on the balcony or argues about fútbol with my brother-in-law. But it's not fake either. It's the part of me that learned to operate in a system that wasn't built for people like me. A Colombian who came here with an accent and a degree nobody recognized at first.

Maybe I resented it for a long time because it felt like the system was asking me to become someone else. But writing that self-assessment—I wasn't lying. I was translating. And translation isn't betrayal. My papá spoke three regional dialects. He didn't lose himself switching between them.

Submitted my director application packet today. Fernando reviewed it yesterday and said it was solid. Included the self-assessment, three peer endorsements, and a one-page proposal for how I'd restructure the backend org.

The proposal was the hardest part. I had to suggest changes to the current structure, which means implicitly criticizing how things are done now. I kept it diplomatic—framed everything as "building on existing strengths" rather than pointing out problems. Ana said I should be bolder. Maybe she's right. But I know who's on the review panel, and bold doesn't play well with everyone.

Coached volleyball practice after work. The girls are getting better at their rotations. They're learning to trust the system—each person covers their zone, and the whole court gets covered. There's a lesson there that I keep noticing but I'm not going to spell it out.

Nudge: "You keep it diplomatic—what are you protecting?"

Interview panel was today. Four people, ninety minutes. I said what I prepared. Didn't improvise, didn't overshoot. Answered the hard question about team conflict honestly—told them I default to listening first. They nodded. Whether that's enough, I'll know in two weeks. Going to bed early. Camila left soup on the stove.

Picked up a bubble tea order from Toa Payoh and the auntie at the shop asked if I ever get tired of riding around all day. Told her it beats sitting in an air-conditioned office watching my soul leak out through Excel spreadsheets. She laughed, said her son is trying to get her to hire a manager so she can rest. "Rest is for the dead," she said. I think about that sometimes—how some people need the motion, the doing, even when the body is telling them to stop.

Delivered to a condo in Newton. The guy who took the order was maybe mid-twenties, looked like he hadn't slept in days. Developer, probably. I remember that life—the midnight oil, the takeout containers stacking up. He tipped twenty bucks, which is rare. Made me wonder if he saw something in me, recognized the uniform of someone who opted out of the grind. Or maybe he was just too tired to count properly.

The thing about this work is you see everyone's lives in fragments. A birthday cake for a kid in Clementi. Medicine from a pharmacy to an elderly woman in Bedok who asked me to wait while she found exact change. Each delivery is a tiny window into something bigger. I don't know if I'm helping or just moving products around. Both, maybe. The city needs its circulatory system.

Nudge: "What do you mean by "opted out of the grind"?"

Response: Opted out is probably too clean a phrase for what happened. I didn't make some grand declaration. After the restructuring, I took a few weeks off, started driving for Grab "temporarily," and then the temporary turned permanent. The truth is I was scared to go back. Scared I'd slip back into caring about quarterly targets and performance reviews. Scared I'd become someone who measures his worth in promotions again. So I kept riding. Five years now. Priya says I'm running from something. Maybe she's right. But at least on the road, I'm running toward something too—even if I don't know what it is yet.

Brother-in-law made a comment at family dinner about "still doing delivery." I told him not everyone needs a business card to feel important. He didn't say anything after that, but Priya gave me that look.

Nudge: "What was that look about?"

Response: The look that says I embarrassed her in front of her family again. That I could have just let it go instead of making things awkward. She's right, probably. But something in me couldn't let him sit there feeling superior. He sells insurance—half his job is convincing people to be afraid of the future. At least when I hand someone their food, they're genuinely happy to see me.

Spent the morning at the shelter helping sort donations—winter clothes that people send even though we're in the tropics, which says something about how little attention donors pay but also something about the impulse to give, that even misguided giving is still a reaching-out. Ramesh was there, the Bangladeshi construction worker I've been teaching English to on Saturdays. His vocabulary is getting better. Today he said "bureaucracy" correctly and I felt stupidly proud.

Then back on the bike by 2pm for the dinner rush. The contrast is jarring sometimes—one moment you're sitting with men who send half their salary home to families they see once every two years, the next you're delivering premium sashimi to someone who complains the wasabi is on the wrong side of the box. I try not to judge. Everyone is fighting something. The sashimi guy might be going through a divorce for all I know. But still.

Route took me through Geylang. The old shophouses are getting demolished one by one. Another one gone since last month, replaced by scaffolding and a sign promising "luxury living." I don't know why I keep track. It's not like I can do anything about it. But someone should be witnessing it, I think. Someone should notice what we're losing.
Rain all day. Soaked through three times. App crashed twice during peak hours so I lost the surge pricing bonuses. I need to remember days like this when I romanticize "freedom from the cubicle."

Twelve-hour shift but hit my weekly target. Priya and I had dinner together for the first time in two weeks—she made fish curry, the Kerala style her mother taught her. Felt like us again.

Nudge: "What do you mean by "felt like us again"?"

Response: I mean we weren't performing. No tension underneath the conversation about money or my "career choices." Just two people who've been together fifteen years, eating fish curry, talking about nothing important. Her laugh came easy. Mine too. I forget sometimes that this is what I'm working for—not the money itself, but these moments. The ability to sit across from her without apology. To be present without calculating what I should be doing instead.

Year-end party season means everyone wants everything delivered everywhere. Did a corporate gift run today—thirty identical gift baskets to thirty different addresses across the island. Whoever organized this clearly didn't think about the human being who'd have to actually execute it. By basket twenty-two, my back was screaming. Sat in a void deck in Tampines for ten minutes just breathing.

The weird thing is, I had this flash of missing my old job. Not the job itself, but the end-of-year bonuses. The company dinners at nice restaurants. The illusion of security. Priya's been hinting that her sister's husband (yes, the business card guy) might have a position opening at his agency. I said I'd think about it. I'm not sure if I said that to keep the peace or because part of me is actually considering it.

New year. Priya wanted to watch the fireworks at Marina Bay but I couldn't face the crowds. We watched from the rooftop of our HDB instead, the view half-blocked by the neighboring tower. She fell asleep before midnight. I stayed up, counting the distant pops of light.

Made the usual resolutions in my head—save more, drink less, be more patient. But underneath all that, a different question kept surfacing: is this the life I want for another year? Not the delivery work specifically, but the uncertainty, the lack of a plan. I used to think planning was a trap. Now I'm wondering if having no plan is its own kind of trap. The kind where you wake up at fifty still "figuring things out."

Nudge: "What would having a plan even look like for you?"

Response: Honestly, I don't know. That's the problem, isn't it? Every time I try to picture a "plan," it looks like going backward—back to the office, back to the hierarchy, back to measuring life in KPIs. Maybe I just need to find a different kind of plan. One that doesn't feel like surrender.

Someone on a riders' Telegram group posted an article about gig workers "lacking ambition." Had to stop myself from typing a rant. Ambition isn't one-size-fits-all—some of us just refuse to let corporations define what success looks like.

Priya finally said it out loud: she's scared. Not angry, not disappointed—scared. About what happens if I get injured, if the gig economy regulations change, if we never have enough for a real retirement. I wanted to argue, to list all the reasons this life is worth the risk. But looking at her face, I couldn't.

The thing is, I know she's not wrong. I've been telling myself that freedom is worth the instability, that I'm building something even if it doesn't look like what everyone else is building. But am I? Or am I just afraid of commitment dressed up as philosophy? The men at the shelter—they don't have the luxury of choosing instability. They're here because they have no choice. I'm here because I walked away from choices. There's a difference, and I'm not sure it's a flattering one.

Rode around Jurong West after we talked, just to clear my head. Ended up at a hawker center, eating prata and watching families come and go. A father helping his daughter with homework. An elderly couple sharing a plate of carrot cake. Ordinary scenes. The kind of life that requires showing up, day after day, even when it's boring. Especially when it's boring. Maybe that's its own kind of courage. Maybe I've been confusing restlessness with meaning.

Spent most of the day doing Deliveroo runs in the rain. The app kept sending me to Kralingen and back, which eats petrol. Made about seventy euros after costs. Not terrible, not good.

Drove past the old Fenix warehouse on the way home. They've turned it into some kind of food hall now. Mam and I used to go there when I was small — it was still a working warehouse then, real dockworkers loading ships. She'd bring me along when she visited Tante Lien. Strange seeing the same walls but everything inside different. The bones are there but the life has changed.

Called Mam tonight. She asked when I'm coming for Sunday. Told her this week looks difficult because of a booking in Dordrecht. She didn't say anything but the silence was louder than words.

Nudge: "What was it about that warehouse that stayed with you?"

Long day. Did a furniture assembly job in Capelle through Werkspot, then four hours of deliveries in the evening. My back is killing me. Should probably see someone about it but I don't have the coverage for a physiotherapist right now. The basic insurance only goes so far.

Had a strange moment at the assembly job. The couple who hired me — young, maybe early thirties — they were hanging family photos on the wall of their new flat. Grandparents, wedding photos, kids at christenings. The man said something about wanting the living room to feel like his grandmother's house. I understood that completely. But then he also had this app controlling all the lights and the thermostat and I thought, you can't have your oma's living room run by a computer. It doesn't work like that.

Too tired to think about it more. Eating leftover stamppot and going to bed.

Nudge: "Sounds like that bothered you more than just being tired."

Response: Maybe. It's not about the smart lights, really. It's that everyone wants the feeling of the old way without actually living it. They want the photos on the wall and the gezelligheid but they also want the algorithm to set the temperature. You can't curate warmth. My grandmother's house was warm because she was there all day, baking, mending, talking to the neighbours. It wasn't a setting you could select. I think what bothers me is that I understand the impulse — I want it too — but I can see it slipping into something hollow. And I'm not sure I'm doing any better. I drive around all day for an app. My father would not have understood this life at all.

Can't sleep so writing again. Mind is jumping around. Listened to a recording of the Zeeland dialect my cousin Jaap posted in the family WhatsApp group — some old farmer telling a story about the flood of '53. My grandfather survived that flood. He never talked about it directly but it shaped everything about how he lived afterward. Every decision was about making sure the family had solid ground.

That's what I keep coming back to. Solid ground. My grandfather rebuilt after the water took the house. My father kept the shop going for four decades in the same spot. And here I am checking an app to see if someone in Rotterdam-Zuid wants their Pad Thai delivered before it gets cold.

But tonight something small happened that felt right. One of my regular delivery customers — an old woman on Nieuwe Binnenweg — she recognised me and asked me to come in for a moment. She'd made a pot of coffee and had nobody to drink it with. I said I had five minutes. We talked about the weather and she showed me a photo of her late husband. I was fifteen minutes late on my next delivery and got a warning from the app. Don't care.

Sitting with her felt like the most honest thing I'd done all week. Like something my father would have done without even thinking about it.

Missed Koningsdag with the family because I took a full day of bookings. Needed the money. Mam sent photos of everyone at Uncle Willem's — the kids in orange, Opa's flag on the pole like always. My niece asked where I was. I hate that she had to ask. I should have been there. The money isn't worth what I gave up.

Saturday. Drove to Zeeland after my morning shifts. Made it in time for coffee and cake at Mam's. She'd kept the leftover appeltaart from Wednesday's church group. We sat in the kitchen and she told me stories about Dad fixing the shop's awning every spring. Fell asleep on her couch before dinner. Woke up feeling more rested than I have in weeks.

Nudge: "What was different about that rest?"

Response: It's the house. The sounds. The clock in the hall that's been ticking since before I was born. Mam's radio on low in the kitchen. No notifications, no app telling me where to go next. Just the shape of a day I recognise from childhood. My body knows how to rest there because nothing in that house is trying to get my attention. Everything is just where it's been for thirty years.

Had a conversation with my ex-wife Anneke today. She called about logistics for our son Bram's birthday next week. Bram's turning twenty-two. We kept it practical — who's bringing what, what time, where. But at the end she said something that stuck. She said, "You sound tired, Gerrit. Not just body tired."

She's not wrong. I've been thinking about what my life looks like from the outside and whether it matches what I thought it would be at this age. My father at fifty-one had the shop, the house, the family under one roof, the church council seat. He knew where he'd be every day for the next ten years. I don't know where I'll be next Tuesday.

I'm not envious exactly. He also had worries I don't have — inventory, rent, employees. But he had a shape to his life. A pattern you could trace. Mine feels like scattered dots.

The strange part is that within the gig work there are small rituals I've built. Same route through Delfshaven in the mornings. Same café for my break. Same nod to the guy at the parking garage. It's not nothing. But it's not the same as what was handed down to me. I'm making something, but I can't tell yet if it holds.

Nudge: "What would it take for those rituals to feel like enough?"

Response: Honestly, I think it would take other people sharing them. My father's routines meant something because the whole family moved through them together. Opening the shop, closing the shop, dinner at six, church on Sunday — these weren't just his habits, they were ours. My café stop in Delfshaven is mine alone. Nobody notices if I skip it. That's the difference. A ritual that only you observe is just a habit. I think for it to hold, someone else would have to care whether I kept it or not.

Bram's birthday dinner tonight. Anneke organised it at that Indonesian place he likes. We were all there — Anneke, her partner Maarten, Bram, Bram's girlfriend, me. Could have been awkward. Wasn't, really.

Bram asked me to say grace before the meal. Nobody else at the table does that anymore. Anneke looked surprised. Maarten looked at his plate. But Bram asked, so I did. Kept it short. Thanked God for the food and for Bram being healthy and here with us. My voice cracked a bit on the last part but I don't think anyone noticed.

After dinner, Bram walked me to my car and said, "Thanks for doing that, Pa. Oma would have liked it." He's right. She would have. That one sentence carried me all the way home.

Got a notification today that Deliveroo is changing the payment structure again. Lower base rate, higher "surge" bonuses. Which means they want us competing against each other for the busy slots instead of earning a steady amount. Every few months they adjust something and we just have to absorb it. There's no negotiation. There's no union rep to call. There's a feedback form that goes nowhere.

My father would have walked out. He walked out of a supplier deal once because the man changed the terms after a handshake. Said if a man's word doesn't hold, his product won't either. That was the end of it. He found another supplier that afternoon. I can't find another Deliveroo. I mean, I can switch to Uber Eats or Thuisbezorgd, but they all do the same thing. There's no door to walk out of because there are no walls — just an app you log into.

I talked to another driver at the pickup point about it. He shrugged. He's twenty-four. He doesn't think about it the way I do. For him this is temporary — a step between university and a real job. For me this is the job. This is Monday. This is how I pay rent.

I'm not angry at him. I'm angry that I'm fifty-one and standing in the same spot as a twenty-four-year-old with a completely different horizon. We're doing the same work but living in different stories.

Went to church this morning. Not my usual one in Rotterdam — I drove to Zeeland for the service at the old church in Goes. Sat in the same pew where my family has sat for as long as anyone remembers. Third row, left side, near the pillar.

Pastor de Vries preached about the parable of the sower. Seeds on rocky ground, seeds among thorns, seeds in good soil. I've heard it a hundred times. But this time I thought about it differently. The rocky ground isn't always a place — sometimes it's a season. Sometimes the soil is fine but the weather is wrong. You still plant.

After the service, old Meneer Bakker shook my hand and asked about my mother. Said he remembered my father fondly. "A man who kept his word and kept his shop clean." That's two things, but they capture Dad pretty well.

Drove back to Rotterdam in the afternoon. The highway was busy, everyone returning from the weekend. I had a delivery booking at four but I took the long route through the country roads instead, past the fields and the old dykes. Got to Rotterdam ten minutes late. Didn't rush.

I don't have the life my father had. I might never have it. But I can still sit in that pew and drive those roads and remember that the ground under all of this is the same ground. It doesn't move, even when I do.

Nudge: "What does that ground hold for you right now?"

Response: Family. The language. The church. The way Mam still sets the table the same way every Sunday. The fact that Bram asked me to say grace, which means something got passed along even through the mess of the divorce and the years I wasn't fully present. The ground is whatever doesn't change based on what app I open or what rate I get paid. It's the part of me that existed before any of this and will still be there after. I can't always stand on it — sometimes I'm too far away, too busy, too caught up in the week. But knowing it's there is what keeps me from feeling completely adrift.

Got an email from the procurement office in Mafraq saying they want to switch to a cheaper Chinese filter unit. Half the cost, sure, but the failure rate data I have seen from field tests in East Africa is not good. I started drafting a reply with the test results attached, then stopped. Because what if they just need to save money this quarter and I am the one being difficult.

Rania said at dinner that Noor needs new textbooks and the car registration is due. She did not say it as a complaint, just facts, and that somehow made it worse. I kept thinking about those filters in Mafraq and whether I am fighting the right fight or just being stubborn about something that is not mine to decide.

Nudge: "What makes it feel like stubbornness vs. the right call?"

Response: When it is the right call I feel steady about it. This time I keep second-guessing, which probably means I know the cheaper option might actually work fine and I just hate the idea of cutting corners on something that goes to people who cannot complain if it breaks.

Cannot sleep. Went back to my laptop after everyone was in bed and found a message from Youssef, my old university friend who still works at the consultancy I left. He forwarded a job listing -- senior engineering director, Dubai office, salary that would cover both girls' tuition in one year. He wrote "just in case" with a smiley face. I know he means well.

The thing that got me was not the money. It was the simplicity. Show up, solve technical problems, go home. Nobody asking me to justify why clean water matters more than a lower unit cost. Nobody expecting me to write another grant narrative about "impact metrics" while families in Azraq are drinking from storage tanks that have not been serviced in months. I sat there staring at the listing for maybe twenty minutes.

Then I closed the tab. But I did not delete the email, and I am not sure what that means. Rania would not pressure me either way, but she would be relieved, and I can picture exactly how her shoulders would drop. That image keeps coming back. I feel like I chose this path and I keep choosing it, but tonight the choosing feels heavier than usual.

Nudge: "Why didn't you delete it?"

Response: Because part of me wanted to keep the option visible. Not to take it, probably. But to know it exists. Like proof that I could still walk away if this gets too hard. That sounds pathetic written down.

Drove out to the factory in Sahab this morning. Watched the team assemble thirty units in two hours, tested every seal myself. Loaded them into the truck for the Irbid delivery and the driver, Abu Faris, said his cousin's family has one of our filters and the kids stopped getting sick last winter.

Board call this afternoon, mostly routine. Layla from the Beirut office mentioned a school in Bekaa that wants to pilot our units in their water system. My mind wandered to whether we could scale the housing design to fit institutional pipes.

Nudge: "Did you bring up the school idea on the call?"

Response: No. I have learned not to pitch things until I have run the numbers. Last time I jumped ahead on a project in front of the board, the finance guy asked about margins and I had nothing. Embarrassing. Now I keep ideas to myself until they are bulletproof, which means some of them die quietly in my notebook.

A report came out from an NGO I respect showing that three water projects in southern Jordan were suspended due to funding cuts. Three. Just gone. Families who were relying on those systems now have to go back to buying trucked water at five times the cost. I read it on my phone during Leila's basketball game and could not focus on the match after that.

It makes me furious in a way I cannot really explain to anyone at the game. The other parents are talking about weekend plans and I am sitting there doing math in my head about how many households that affects. Rania noticed and squeezed my hand but did not ask. She knows the look by now.

Nudge: "Does Rania ever say anything about the look?"

Met with a potential corporate sponsor today, a petrochemical company that wants to fund fifty filter units for a camp near Zarqa as part of their CSR portfolio. Good meeting on paper. They want their logo on the units, photos for their annual report, a "brand ambassador" visit where executives tour the camp.

I said yes to all of it because we need the money. Fifty units is fifty families with clean water, and that is not nothing. But I spent the drive back feeling uneasy. The camp is not a backdrop. The people there are not props for someone's sustainability page. I know how it works -- I have done these partnerships before and the water still gets delivered -- but every time I shake hands on one of these deals I lose a small piece of something.

Told Rania about it over tea and she said, "You always do this. You take the deal and then you punish yourself for taking it." She is probably right. I just wish there was a way to fund this work that did not require performing gratitude for people who are writing it off on their taxes.

Noor asked me at dinner why we do not live in a bigger house like her friend Dina's family. She is fifteen and it was not a complaint, more like genuine curiosity. I told her we chose to put our money into the business. She asked what the business does exactly, and I realized I have never really sat down and explained it to her properly.

So I did. I told her about the filters, about the camps, about how water-borne illness is the thing that keeps kids out of school more than anything else in those communities. She listened, then asked, "But does it actually change anything or does it just help for a while?" I did not have a quick answer for that. It is the question I carry around constantly and hearing it from my daughter in her school uniform at our kitchen table hit differently than when it comes from a grant reviewer.

I said something about how helping for a while still matters. She shrugged and went to do homework. I sat there for a long time after. The honest answer is I do not know if what I am building will outlast the next funding cycle, and some nights that uncertainty feels like it is eating through the floor under me. But I also cannot imagine doing anything else, which maybe is its own kind of trap.

Long day at the factory fixing a seal press that jammed. Hands smell like silicone and my back is done. But we got the Zarqa order out on time and Abu Faris brought his wife's knafeh to share with the crew, which made the overtime feel less grim.

My brother Faisal called to tell me our cousin Samir just got promoted to VP at his bank in Riyadh. I do not need validation from Faisal. He has never understood why I walked away from the consultancy and he is never going to. What I do know is that last week a mother in Irbid told our field coordinator that her son's eczema cleared up two months after we installed the filter. That is real. Samir's title is a line on a business card. I should not have to keep defending this to myself but apparently I still do.

Started the new term today. My Primary 3 class has 32 students this year, and already I can tell Jayden in the back row is going to be a handful. Had a meeting with Mrs. Tan, my mentor, about my classroom management plan.

She said my lesson on fractions last week was "creative but needed tighter pacing." I stayed until 7pm revising the slides. Arjun texted asking if we're still going for Korean BBQ on Saturday - I said yes but I'm already thinking about how much marking I need to clear.

Nudge: "You mentioned saying yes to Korean BBQ but your mind went straight to marking. What's making you think about work when you're making weekend plans?"

I've been thinking about something Mrs. Tan said during our feedback session. She mentioned that I spend more time on "fun activities" than other beginning teachers and asked if I was "prioritizing student engagement over curriculum coverage." It stung more than it should have.

The thing is, my kids actually look forward to my lessons. Aisha told me yesterday that math is her favorite subject now because of my fraction pizza activity. Or maybe they're just faster because they've been doing it longer. I don't know.

Nudge: "That comment from Mrs. Tan clearly landed hard. What is it about her feedback that got under your skin?"

Response: Honestly, I think I'm just tired. It's the end of term and everyone's stressed. She probably didn't mean anything by it - she's been teaching for 20 years and has seen a hundred new teachers come through. I'll focus on pacing next term and it'll be fine.

Last day of term. I just got home and it's 9pm. End-of-year reports took forever because I insisted on writing personalized comments for all 32 students instead of using the template phrases. Mrs. Tan actually complimented my reports today - said the parents would appreciate the detail. Small win, I guess.

But I'm so tired. Arjun and I were supposed to go to the Christmas market at Gardens by the Bay tonight but I cancelled. He was understanding but I could hear the disappointment. This is the third thing I've cancelled this month. Last week was my cousin Meera's birthday dinner, and before that it was supposed to be a spa day with my college friends.

Everyone keeps telling me the first year is the hardest. My colleague Rachel, who's in her second year, said she barely saw her husband for six months. Is that supposed to make me feel better? Mum called yesterday and when I told her I was exhausted, she said "but this is what you wanted, no?" And yes, I did want this. I love being in the classroom. Today during our class party, watching the kids exchange cards and eat cookies, I felt so happy. Dinesh, my quietest student, actually came up and gave me a handmade card that said "Best Teacher."

I just wish I could have both - be good at this job AND have a life outside of it. Right now it feels like I'm choosing one over the other and somehow still failing at both.

Nudge: "You said it feels like you're failing at both work and life outside it. But you also mentioned getting compliments on your reports and a handmade card from a student. What would 'not failing' actually look like to you?"

Response: I think not failing would mean... being present. When I'm at work, fully at work without guilt about what I'm missing. And when I'm with Arjun or my friends, actually being there instead of mentally going through my to-do list. Right now I'm always half somewhere else. The card from Dinesh was beautiful but even in that moment part of me was thinking about how I still needed to input grades into the portal.

Christmas Eve. Arjun and I are at his parents' place in Johor for the long weekend. His mum made mutton biryani and there's way too much food as usual. His younger sister Kavitha is back from Melbourne so there's been a lot of catching up.

We went to a night market yesterday and I bought these ridiculous matching couple keychains shaped like curry puffs. Arjun pretended to be embarrassed but I caught him showing his sister later. Today we just lazed around - watched a movie, played cards with his grandma, ate leftovers for lunch. His parents have this swing seat in their garden and I spent an hour there just reading a novel. An actual novel, not a teaching resource.

The contrast from last week is stark. I haven't thought about school once today. Well, except when Kavitha asked about my job and I ended up talking about Dinesh's card for ten minutes. But that was nice talking, not stressed thinking.

Arjun asked if I was feeling better. I said yes. He said "good, you looked really worn out" and honestly, I hadn't realized how bad it had gotten until I stepped away from it. Tomorrow we're driving to Malacca for the day to try this famous chicken rice ball place his colleague recommended.

Back in Singapore. The Malacca trip was lovely - we walked through Jonker Street, ate cendol, took photos by those colorful murals. Arjun said we should do more spontaneous trips like this. I agreed, but already my mind is drifting to school reopening next week.

I've been thinking about what "presence" means. Over the past few days I felt like myself again - laughing at dumb jokes, being curious about things that aren't work-related, enjoying food without rushing. But now that I'm home, sitting in my apartment with my laptop open, I can feel the familiar anxiety creeping back. I haven't opened my email yet but I know there will be things waiting. Part of me wants to check "just to know what's there." Part of me wants to protect this feeling a little longer.

Nudge: "You're sitting with the choice of whether to open your email right now. What do you think would happen if you waited until tomorrow?"

Response: Probably nothing catastrophic. School doesn't officially start until next Wednesday. But there's this voice that says I'll be behind, that other teachers are already preparing, that Mrs. Tan might have sent feedback I need to act on. The rational part of me knows one more day won't matter. The anxious part is already composing a to-do list.

I'm going to close the laptop. Tomorrow is soon enough.

First week back and I'm already feeling the pressure. Today Mrs. Tan called me into her office to discuss my "development goals" for the year. She brought up the MOE promotion framework and asked where I saw myself in five years. I panicked and said "senior teacher track" because that's what you're supposed to say, right?

But sitting here now, I'm not even sure that's true. The senior teachers I see are in meetings constantly, doing administrative work, mentoring others. They're hardly ever in classrooms with kids anymore. And isn't that the whole point? Isn't that why I became a teacher?

I called my friend Meilin who teaches at another school. She's the same age but already coordinating the Science department's enrichment programs. She sounded excited about it - talked about "visibility" and "building a portfolio." When I told her about feeling conflicted, she said "Priya, you can't stay a classroom teacher forever. That's just how the system works."

So now I'm confused and also a little annoyed. Why can't I just be good at teaching? Why does everything have to be about climbing some ladder? I keep thinking about Dinesh and how he's finally starting to participate in class. That matters to me. More than being on some committee or getting a title.

But then there's Mum and Papa, who video called on New Year's Day asking about my "career progression." And Arjun's parents, who are so proud that both their kids have "professional jobs." And Mrs. Tan, who genuinely wants to help me succeed by her definition of success.

I feel like I'm disappointing everyone by wanting something simpler.

Nudge: "You mentioned feeling like you're disappointing everyone by wanting something simpler. But who exactly decides what 'success' means for your career - them or you?"

Response: That's a loaded question. In theory, me. In practice, there are real consequences to not playing the game - slower pay progression, fewer opportunities, being seen as lacking drive. It's not as simple as "follow your heart" when the system is designed around a specific path.

Something good happened today and I want to remember it. We started a new unit on creative writing and I let the kids write about anything they wanted. Most wrote about their holidays, pets, favorite games. But Aisha - the one who said math was her favorite subject - wrote this whole story about a girl who could fly but chose to walk because she liked how flowers smelled at ground level.

I pulled her aside after class to tell her how much I loved it. She got so shy and happy at the same time. Her mom picked her up today and I mentioned the story - she looked surprised, said Aisha usually hates writing.

Moments like this are why I teach. Not for reviews or promotions or what it looks like on paper. For a 9-year-old discovering she can create something beautiful with words. I don't know how to put that in a "development goal" but it's the truest thing I know about myself right now.

Long Sunday. Arjun and I had brunch at Atlas then walked around Bugis. Nice day but my mind kept wandering to tomorrow, to meetings, to whether I'm doing enough.

Nudge: "You mentioned wondering if you're doing enough. Enough for what, or for whom?"

Response: For myself, I think. Or the version of myself I thought I'd be by now. When I was in NIE, I imagined being this effortlessly great teacher who also had time for friends and hobbies and travel. Instead I'm constantly choosing between being mediocre at everything or good at one thing while the rest falls apart. Today was supposed to be relaxing but I couldn't stop calculating how many hours until Monday. That's not who I want to be.

Twelve-hour shift and my feet are just wrecked. But honestly a decent day. One of my kids on the ward, Milo, he's seven and has been in and out for weeks with a recurring infection. Today he finally got the all-clear for discharge. His mom was crying and I was trying not to cry and he just kept asking if he could keep the stickers I'd been putting on his IV pole every morning.

I told him yes obviously. Grabbed a handful of extras from the supply closet and stuffed them in a ziplock for him. His mom hugged me on the way out and said I was the only nurse who remembered he was scared of the blood pressure cuff and always warned him first. I do remember that stuff. It matters.

Got home and Mom had made mac and cheese from a box, which she was very proud of. Her hip is doing better, she managed the kitchen without the walker. We ate on the couch watching Chopped reruns. I didn't have the energy to make anything real. But sitting there with the mac and cheese and Mom laughing at the contestants, I just felt like okay. This is fine. This is actually good.

Nudge: "Is that what good usually looks like?"

Lisa from work asked me again about the charge nurse thing. Said the manager is "disappointed" I turned it down and that I'm "wasting my potential."
