I told her I appreciate the concern but I'm good where I am. She gave me that look, the one where people think you're being lazy or afraid when really you just know what works for you.

I'm not afraid of more responsibility. I covered the entire unit by myself that time two nurses called out during the flu surge. I can handle it. I just don't want to lose my Saturdays and trade my shift for nights. Mom needs someone home in the evenings and I need at least a couple days a month where I do absolutely nothing useful. That's not a character flaw.

Nudge: "Does Lisa's opinion actually get to you or is it just annoying?"

Response: It gets to me more than I want it to. She's not wrong that I could do the job. I just don't want it right now. But hearing "wasting potential" sticks in my head longer than it should.

Mom had a rough night, woke up at 3am with hip pain and I ended up sitting with her for two hours doing ice packs and finding the right pillow arrangement. Then went straight to work at 6:30. Running on maybe three hours of sleep.

The thing that's eating at me is that at one point she was apologizing for waking me up and I snapped. Not badly, just a quick "Mom it's fine, please stop saying sorry." But I could hear the edge in my voice and she went quiet. She didn't deserve that. She didn't ask for any of this. I just — I was so tired and I had work in a few hours and the frustration came out sideways.

Day off. Slept in until 10 which felt almost criminal. Mom was already up, puttering around with her walker, watching the morning news at a volume that could wake the neighborhood. Made us both a proper breakfast — scrambled eggs with chives from the little herb pot on the windowsill, sourdough toast, fresh orange juice. She said it was better than the diner we used to go to on Sundays when I was little. I don't think that's true but it was sweet.

Spent the afternoon doing laundry and reorganizing the hall closet, which has been bothering me for weeks. Mom sat in her chair and directed me on where everything should go, like she was running a construction site. It was actually kind of fun. Found a box of old photos from when my brother Kevin and I were small. Mom got teary looking at them but in a happy way.

Kevin called in the evening. He lives in Denver now. Asked how Mom was doing and I gave him the update. He said he's sorry he can't help more and that he'll try to visit in August. I said that would be great, and I meant it, but I also know "try" usually means it won't happen. I'm not angry about it. He has his own life, two kids, a wife with a demanding job. It just is what it is. I'm the one who's here and that's the arrangement we've settled into.

Nudge: "When Kevin says 'try to visit' — what do you actually expect?"

Response: Honestly? Nothing. And I've made my peace with that mostly. He'll call, send money sometimes, maybe show up for Thanksgiving. But the day-to-day stuff is mine. It's been that way since Mom's surgery and probably won't change.

Picked up an extra shift for Dani because her kid had a fever. Sixteen hours on my feet. I can barely think straight right now. Got home and Mom had waited up for me even though it was almost midnight. She'd left a plate of pasta in the microwave with a little note that said "eat something, mija."

I feel bad because my Saturday is supposed to be this weekend and I don't think I can do it. Mom has a follow-up appointment Saturday morning and Kevin was supposed to call her PT coordinator back last week and never did, so now I have to sort that out too. I was going to do the sushi-and-movie thing. I've been looking forward to it all week. But I keep giving those days away and then wondering why I feel like a wrung-out dishrag.

Nudge: "What if you kept the Saturday anyway?"

Called Kevin about the PT coordinator thing. He apologized, said it slipped his mind. I told him it's fine but could he at least handle the insurance paperwork that's due next week. He said sure. We'll see.

I rescheduled Mom's appointment to Monday and I'm keeping my Saturday. I made the decision and I feel weird about it. Like I'm being selfish even though rationally I know a two-day delay on a routine follow-up doesn't matter. But there's this voice in my head that sounds a lot like Mom's voice — not the real Mom, the imaginary guilt-trip Mom — saying I should just handle it and stop complaining. Real Mom would probably tell me to go enjoy my day. She's not the problem. I'm the problem.

Took my Saturday. Hot bath, ordered the good sushi from Kiko's, watched that French movie I've had saved for a month. Nobody needed anything from me for eight whole hours and I didn't check my phone once.

Nudge: "How'd it feel compared to what you expected?"

Response: Better. Genuinely better. I kept waiting for the guilt to kick in and it mostly didn't. Just felt like myself again.

Took Mom to the rescheduled follow-up. PT says she's progressing well, might be able to transition to outpatient therapy in a couple months. That would free up a lot of evenings for me which I didn't let myself think about too much while the doctor was talking, but it was the first thing that popped into my head.

Started thinking about what I'd do with those evenings. Join a pottery class or something, go to that wine bar Hannah keeps inviting me to. Then immediately felt gross about it because my mom is still recovering and here I am mentally redecorating my free time. I don't know why I can't just want two things at once without feeling like one of them makes me a bad person.

Nudge: "Who told you wanting your own life makes you bad?"

The department meeting today went as expected. Principal wanted us to adopt the new digital assessment platform by January. Several younger teachers were enthusiastic, but I noticed Meera sitting silently, looking overwhelmed. She is only in her second year and already struggling with the curriculum changes from last term.

After the meeting, I walked with her to the staff room and showed her how to break down the transition into smaller steps. She seemed relieved. I did not mention this to anyone else because it might embarrass her. These small moments make me feel I am contributing something worthwhile, even if no one else notices.

Amma's blood pressure was high again this morning. Cancelled my NGO session to take her to the clinic. Feeling stretched thin.

Nudge: "What's weighing on you most about today?"

Response: The children at the tutoring center were expecting me. I had promised to bring the geometry worksheets I prepared. Now another volunteer will have to scramble, and the children will wonder why I didn't come. At the same time, Amma needed me and there was no question about staying. But sitting in that clinic waiting room, I kept thinking about those children waiting too, and feeling like I was failing someone no matter what I chose.

Staff lunch today, celebrating Lakshmi ma'am's retirement after thirty-five years. Everyone contributed dishes, told stories. The principal made a speech about her dedication. I sat there eating my rice and watching her accept a shawl and a plaque, thinking about how she spent three decades in the same building, teaching the same subject, attending the same festivals. She looked content.

My brother called afterward asking when I am planning to retire. His son is getting married next year and he wants to know if I can take extended leave to help with arrangements. I said I would see what is possible. The truth is I have not thought seriously about retirement. Teaching still feels meaningful most days, and I do not know what I would do with empty hours. But my brother sounded like he was already counting on my availability, and I did not want to disappoint him by saying I prefer to keep working.

There is also the question of my pension and whether the timing would be right. I have been careful with money but there are always uncertainties. Maybe I should start considering it more seriously. The conversation left me feeling unsettled in a way I cannot quite name.

Nudge: "You mentioned not wanting to disappoint your brother. What about what you want?"
Finished grading the Class 10 exams. Most students improved from last term. Ravi, who was failing in September, scored 72%.

My colleague Anand said something today that I cannot stop thinking about. We were discussing the new reservation policy for faculty hiring, and he made a dismissive comment about how it lowers standards. I disagreed but only said "these policies have complex reasons" and changed the subject to exam schedules.

Later I felt angry at myself. I have taught students from every background for nearly three decades and I know that talent is not distributed by birth. But I did not say any of this to Anand. I worried about creating an awkward atmosphere in the department. Now I am sitting here wondering if my silence made it seem like I agreed. The children I tutor on weekends would not recognize this version of me, the one who says nothing when it matters.

Nudge: "You mentioned the children wouldn't recognize this version of you. Which version feels more like the real one?"

New year, same routines. I find comfort in this. The school reopened today and there is something settling about walking through the gates, hearing the morning assembly, smelling chalk and floor cleaner.

I brought sweets for the staff room, as I do every January. Small gestures maintain the fabric of a workplace. I noticed the new Hindi teacher, Priya, looking uncertain during break time. I invited her to sit with our group. She seemed grateful. I remember how difficult my first year was, how much it mattered when senior teachers acknowledged me. If I can make someone's path a little easier, that feels like enough.

Nudge: "What do you remember most about that first year?"

Response: I remember feeling like a fraud. I had my qualifications, my training, but standing in front of a classroom full of fourteen-year-olds who could sense every hesitation, I felt exposed. There was one day, maybe three months in, when a student asked me a question I could not answer. I panicked, gave some vague response, and spent the whole night convinced everyone knew I was not qualified to be there.

What saved me was Mrs. Subramanian, who taught physics. She never made a fuss, just started including me in her lunch conversations, asking my opinion on small matters, treating me like I belonged. I never told her how much that meant. She passed away six years ago. I suppose I keep doing for others what she did for me, hoping it matters the way it mattered to me.

My sister-in-law visited today and made a comment about how I spend too much time on "outside activities" instead of focusing on the family. She was referring to the NGO work. I explained calmly that I only go twice a month and always ensure Amma is cared for. She nodded but I could see she was not convinced.

I should not have to justify myself. I have managed this household for fifteen years while working full-time. I have never once complained about cooking for her when she visits or adjusting my schedule around her preferences. The tutoring gives me purpose beyond daily obligations. But I did not say any of this. I just served tea and changed the subject to her daughter's college applications.

Nudge: "What does that purpose feel like when you're there with the children?"

Response: It feels like my skills matter beyond grades and syllabi. These children, most of them are first-generation learners. Their parents work long hours in construction or domestic help. When I explain a concept and I see understanding appear on a face that was confused a moment before, it is the same satisfaction I felt in my first years of teaching, before it became routine.

At school, I am Padmini ma'am, the department head who files reports and attends meetings. At the tutoring center, I am just someone who knows something useful and is willing to share it. The children do not care about my title or my seniority. They only care that I showed up and that I can help them understand fractions. It is simpler and somehow more honest.

Reading the news this morning about the air quality crisis in Delhi. Schools closed, children wearing masks, hospitals filled with respiratory cases. I kept thinking about the children there who have no choice but to breathe that air, whose families cannot afford air purifiers or trips to cleaner places.

We complain about Chennai traffic and summer heat, but this is different. This is a slow catastrophe that everyone sees but no one seems able to stop. The article mentioned that the poorest neighborhoods have the worst air quality because they are near industrial zones. Of course they do. It is always the same pattern, those with the least resources bearing the heaviest burdens.

I mentioned this to a colleague during lunch and she shrugged, said "what can we do, these things are beyond us." I understand the impulse. The scale of the problem makes individual action feel pointless. But I kept thinking about it through my afternoon classes. The students I teach will inherit this world. What am I preparing them for, if not to care about things larger than their exam scores?

Nudge: "What would it look like to prepare them to care about things larger than exams?"

Response: That is the question, isn't it. I am not sure I have an answer. The syllabus is the syllabus, the exams are the exams. But sometimes I try to use real examples in my word problems. Calculating pollution levels, or ratios of resources. Small things. Maybe it plants a seed, maybe it does not. I cannot solve the crisis but I can teach the children who might.

Difficult day at the tutoring center. One of the regular students, Kavitha, did not come. I asked around and learned her family is moving back to their village because her father lost his job. She is twelve and had been making such progress in mathematics. Now she will attend the government school there, which may not have the same resources.

I keep thinking about all the Kavithas, the children whose potential depends on circumstances they did not choose. I can help a few, a handful each year, but there are so many more. The NGO coordinator asked if I could take on additional sessions. I said I would think about it, but I already know my family obligations make this difficult. Amma needs more care as she gets older, my brother's wedding preparations are starting, and I cannot ask others to carry my responsibilities so I can spend more time elsewhere. But it sits heavily, knowing there are children who need help and I am not able to give it.

Nudge: "What would it feel like to say yes to those additional sessions?"

Response: It would feel like choosing. And I have spent my whole life arranging things so I do not have to choose, so everyone gets enough of me, even if no one gets all of me. If I said yes to more sessions, I would have to say no to something else, and then I would have to explain that no, and see the disappointment, and feel like I am being selfish.

The strange thing is, I know I am not selfish. I have given and given for decades. But somehow the idea of asking for something, of prioritizing what feeds my soul over what is expected, still feels like a transgression. Kavitha's face keeps appearing in my mind. I cannot save her from moving away, but maybe I could have helped more children like her if I had been braver about asking for time that was truly mine.

Look, the pivot makes sense. Everyone at the Thursday pitch night kept asking why we dropped the furniture line when we had traction, but traction is not the same thing as momentum. We had retailers interested, sure, but the margins were getting squeezed and the whole supply chain was becoming a nightmare with shipping costs out of Shenzhen. I am not going to sit there manufacturing the same shelving unit for the next ten years just because some shops in Eindhoven like them.

Pieter thinks I pulled the trigger too fast. He said that in the meeting, in front of Anouk and Daan, which I did not appreciate. He has been with me since the Philips days and sometimes he acts like that gives him permission to question every call I make publicly. Maybe it does. I do not know. But I also know that if I had listened to the cautious voices three years ago we would still be making Bluetooth speakers nobody asked for.

The urban farming kits — there is something there. I can feel it in the conversations I am having. The market is shifting, people want to grow things, they want control over food, they want something tangible. We just need six months to prove the concept. That is not reckless. That is reading the room.

Nudge: "What bothered you most about Pieter saying it?"

Response: That he might be right. Not about the pivot being wrong, but about me not giving things enough time. I have heard it before from other people. It is a pattern I do not love seeing pointed out.

Two days of back-to-back calls with potential suppliers in Portugal and a prototype manufacturer in Ghent. My voice is shot. Ate a cheese sandwich at my desk around three and that was it until I got home and microwaved leftover stamppot that tasted like nothing.

The boys called. Sven wants to go to a music festival in Belgium and needs money. Matteo got a B+ on his physics exam and sounded genuinely proud, which made me proud too, even though I had nothing to do with it. Katrien is the one who sat with him while he studied. I was in Lisbon looking at terracotta planters.

I told Sven I would think about the festival money. What I actually thought was: I cannot keep saying yes to everything just because I feel guilty for not being there. But I will probably say yes anyway because that is what I do — throw money at the gap where my presence should be.

The Ghent manufacturer can do small runs. That is good. Keeps us lean. I should be more excited about that than I am right now. I think I am just tired.

Nudge: "What would being there actually look like?"

Response: I do not know. Sitting in their kitchen helping with homework? Driving Sven to football practice? It sounds so ordinary but I have built my whole life to avoid ordinary, and now ordinary is the thing I cannot reach.

Woke up at four again, brain already running. Kept thinking about whether the modular design should be stackable or side-by-side. Side-by-side takes more floor space but stackable has structural issues — the weight of wet soil plus the grow lights, it is not trivial. Sketched something on the back of an envelope before I even brushed my teeth.

Then my mind jumped to the lease. Studio lease is up in September. Pieter wants to renew for two years. Two years feels like a prison sentence. What if we need to move closer to a port? What if we need less space? What if we need more? I said let us do one year with an option to extend. He gave me that look. The one that says here we go again.

Had coffee with an old colleague from Philips, Joost. He is still there. Same building, same floor, different title. He seemed content but when I described what I was doing his eyes got wide and he said he could never do that. I used to find that flattering. Today it just made me feel alone.

Then back to the planters. Emailed three more suppliers. One in Valencia looks promising. Set up a video call for Friday. Kept going until my neck hurt and I realized it was already dark and I had not eaten. Ordered Thai food and ate it standing in the kitchen scrolling through competitor listings.

Nudge: "Which of those things is actually keeping you up at night?"

Response: The lease. Not because of the space, but because signing anything for a fixed term makes my chest tight. I know that is not rational. It is just how I am wired.

Katrien called. Not the boys, Katrien herself. She never does that unless something is wrong or she needs to tell me something I will not like.

Matteo has been acting out at school. Skipping classes, talking back to teachers. She thinks it started around the time I cancelled the last weekend because of the Lisbon trip. She did not say it is my fault, but she did not have to. The silence after she described what happened was louder than anything she could have said.

I sat in my car for twenty minutes after that call. Just sat there in the parking garage under the studio, engine off, staring at the concrete wall. I wanted to call Matteo but what would I say? Sorry I was looking at planters in Portugal instead of watching your football match? Sorry I keep choosing the next interesting thing over the predictable thing that happens to include you?

He is fourteen. He does not understand that I am trying to build something. Or maybe he understands perfectly and just does not think it is a good enough reason. Maybe he is right.

I am going to Utrecht this weekend. No excuses. I already told Pieter to handle the Friday call with Valencia.

Weekend in Utrecht was hard. Matteo barely looked at me. Sven was fine, acted like nothing was wrong, which somehow felt worse. We went to get frites and walked along the canal and I kept trying to say something meaningful but everything came out flat.

On Sunday I took them to a climbing wall. Matteo actually engaged for the first time. Something about the challenge of it, figuring out the next hold, the height. He was grinning at the top. For about an hour he forgot he was angry with me.

Drove home and cried in the car. Not the dramatic kind, just the quiet leaking kind where you keep driving and wiping your face.

Nudge: "Why do you think the climbing wall worked?"

Response: Because it was not sitting across a table pretending everything is normal. It was doing something together that required focus and a bit of nerve. Maybe that is the only language I know how to speak. Maybe Matteo is the same way and I never noticed.

A whole week gone and I cannot tell you what I accomplished. Meetings, emails, a prototype review that raised more questions than it answered. The Valencia supplier fell through — their minimum order is ten times what we need. Back to square one on that front.

Pieter brought up the furniture line again. Casually, over lunch, like he was just thinking out loud. "We still have the moulds," he said. "The retailers would take us back." I did not respond. Just kept eating my soup.

I spent the evening reading about vertical farming startups in Copenhagen and Stockholm. Some of them are doing incredible things. Modular, scalable, beautiful design. Part of me got fired up. Part of me thought: they are already there. You are late. You are always starting things and by the time you get somewhere, someone else has already built it better.

Anouk sent me a link to a design competition in Milan. Entry deadline is August. I opened the page, looked at the brief, felt that old kick of energy. Then I closed the tab because I know what happens — I spend three weeks on it, neglect the actual business, and end up with a beautiful submission and no revenue.

I do not know what I am doing. I mean I know what I am doing day to day. I just do not know what it adds up to.

Nudge: "What would it need to add up to?"
Took a walk along the Maas after work. Watched the barges pass. Realized I have been in fight mode for weeks and forgot to just look at things. The water helped.

Pieter handed in his notice today. Twenty minutes before our morning standup, just walked into my office and put a printed letter on my desk. After eight years. Eight years of me dragging him through pivots and late nights and "trust me on this one."

He said he has taken a position at a consultancy in Amsterdam. Better pay, predictable hours, no more wondering whether the next quarter will exist. He was calm about it. Almost gentle. Said it was not personal, that he just needs stability, that he has a mortgage and a kid on the way.

I shook his hand and said I understood. And I do. I genuinely do. But the second he left my office I wanted to throw something. Not at him. At the wall, at the situation, at the fact that I am apparently the kind of person people eventually need a break from.

Anouk came in later and asked if I was okay. I said fine. She did not push it. I spent the rest of the day reorganizing the supplier spreadsheet, which did not need reorganizing, because I needed my hands to be doing something that did not require thinking about what happens next.

Katrien would say this is the cost. My mother would say this is the cost. Pieter leaving is just the latest version of someone choosing the stable path while I keep chasing whatever is around the next corner. But what is the alternative? Do what Joost does? Same floor, same desk, different title? I would rather fail at something new every two years than succeed at the same thing for twenty.

That sounds brave when I write it. It does not feel brave right now.

Nudge: "What does it feel like right now?"

Response: Tired, mostly. I will figure it out. I always do. Just need a few days to regroup.

People at the plant keep asking why I don't apply for the regional director position now that Héctor is retiring. I know exactly why — the travel, the relocating, none of that sits right with me. My team runs well, my commute is twenty minutes, and I sleep fine at night.

Nudge: "Is there any part of you that's curious about it?"

Response: Not really. Maybe five years ago I would have thought about it differently, but I watched Héctor miss his daughter's quinceañera because he was in Monterrey for a plant inspection. That told me everything I needed to know.

Lucía mentioned her sister's husband lost his job at the autoparts factory in León. Just like that — twenty-two years and they called a meeting on a Friday afternoon. I checked our savings account twice after dinner, which is not something I normally do on a weekday.

Nudge: "What were you hoping to see when you checked?"

Spent Saturday morning fixing the back fence with my compadre Tomás. Felt good to work with my hands for once instead of staring at spreadsheets. Lucía brought us agua de jamaica and we sat in the yard afterward just talking about nothing important.

Corporate sent an email Friday about "restructuring opportunities" in the operations departments across all three plants. The language was carefully neutral, all about efficiency and modernization, but I have been doing this long enough to recognize what that kind of memo actually means. Somebody is going to lose headcount.

I spent part of Sunday looking through my employment contract — the termination clauses, the severance calculations, the notice periods. Lucía noticed me reading it at the kitchen table instead of doing our usual budget review and asked if something was wrong. I told her I was just being thorough, which is true but also not the whole picture.

The thing that bothers me is not the restructuring itself. Companies do this. What bothers me is that I had filed my contract in the closet three years ago and forgot exactly where. It took me twenty minutes to find it. That should not have happened. I need a better system for keeping important documents where I can reach them quickly.

Nudge: "You spent a lot of time on the contract. What did you find?"

Response: The severance is decent — four months for my tenure — but decent is not the same as comfortable. We could cover things for ten months total if I combined severance with savings, but that assumes nothing else breaks. The car is twelve years old, the roof needs work eventually. Ten months sounds like a lot until you start listing what eats into it.

My direct report Andrés came in excited about some new automation software he wants to pilot on the production line. He showed me a demo and honestly it looked impressive — would probably cut processing time by fifteen percent. But the implementation would mean shutting down Line 2 for three weeks and retraining half the floor staff. Three weeks of lost output during our busiest quarter.

I told him to write up a proposal and I would review it, but the truth is I already know I am going to push it to next fiscal year. Maybe the quarter after that. I could hear how flat my voice sounded when I said it and I think he noticed too.

Nudge: "What made your voice go flat?"

Response: Because I know I am probably being the bottleneck. Andrés is sharp, the software looked solid, and fifteen percent is not nothing. But shutting down a line for three weeks when corporate is already talking about restructuring — that is the kind of disruption that puts a target on your department. I would rather deliver predictable numbers and keep everyone employed than chase an improvement that could backfire at the worst possible time. I just wish I could explain that to Andrés without sounding like I have given up.

Got the water bill and it was almost double the usual amount. Probably a leak somewhere. Called a plumber but the earliest appointment is next Thursday, so I have been turning the main valve off at night just in case.

Plumber came, turned out it was just a faulty meter reading — no actual leak. Lucía laughed about me turning the water off every night for five days. I told her better cautious than flooded, and she kissed my forehead and said "sí, mi amor, así eres tú."

The restructuring memo turned into a town hall meeting this week. They are consolidating some administrative roles at the Querétaro plant but operations at our site stays as-is for now. My boss Carlos pulled me aside afterward and said my numbers were part of the reason they kept Guadalajara intact. Sixteen years of consistent output apparently counts for something.

I reorganized the filing cabinet this weekend. Employment contracts, insurance policies, property deed, car title — all in labeled folders now, easy to grab. Lucía thinks I am being excessive but she also put her own documents in the system without me asking, so I think she sees the point even if she would not say it directly.

Nudge: "How did it feel hearing Carlos say that?"

Response: Relieved, obviously, but also something I did not expect — vindicated, maybe. I have spent years being the manager who says no to flashy ideas, who keeps the same routines, who does not chase promotions. Sometimes I wonder if people think that means I lack ambition. But steady output is what saved those jobs. Not innovation, not bold moves. Just showing up and delivering the same thing every quarter. It is not exciting but it held.

Lucía's mother called this morning to say she wants to sell the family house in Morelia and move into one of those assisted living places. Lucía was quiet for a long time after hanging up. Her mother has lived in that house for forty years — it is where Lucía grew up, where we had our wedding reception in the backyard.

The practical side of it is complicated. Lucía's sister wants to buy the house but does not have enough for a down payment and asked if we could co-sign a loan. I have never co-signed anything in my life. My father told me when I was nineteen: never put your name on someone else's debt, no matter who they are. But this is Lucía's family, and the look on her face when her sister called tonight was not something I can just calculate my way around.

I told them both I needed a few days to think. Lucía did not argue, which means she understands my hesitation even if she hopes I land somewhere different than where I am leaning.

Nudge: "Where are you leaning?"

Response: Toward no. I know how that sounds, and I know what it might cost with Lucía's family. But co-signing means if her sister cannot pay, we pay. And if we pay, that is the emergency fund, that is the roof repair, that is everything I have built to make sure we never end up like Lucía's cuñado in León. I would rather help them find another way — maybe lend a smaller amount directly, something we could absorb if it disappeared. But putting our name on a bank loan is a different thing entirely.

The exhibition proposal went through. Three years of work, and the municipal committee finally said yes. I spent the morning just sitting with the letter, reading it over again. The installation will be about the rising waters—the families in Bangladesh, the villages disappearing, but also what it means here, for us, watching from our safe harbors.

I called my mother to tell her. She asked if it meant I wouldn't have to worry about rent anymore. I said yes, for a while. She sounded relieved. So am I, honestly. The grant covers materials and a modest stipend. It's not wealth, but it's enough to stop checking my account every morning.

Strange to feel both grounded and ambitious at once. Usually those pull in different directions for me.

Spent the whole day researching for the installation and now I can't sleep. The numbers are overwhelming—millions displaced, coastlines eroding, and we're here arguing about parking spaces and bicycle lanes. Not that those don't matter. They do. But the scale of it.

My neighbor Hans stopped by, wanted to borrow sugar of all things, and I ended up talking at him for twenty minutes about sea level projections. Poor man just wanted to bake a cake. He nodded politely and escaped as soon as he could. I do this. I know I do this. The work consumes me and then I become insufferable.

But also: what are we supposed to do? Make art that no one sees? Make art that everyone sees but changes nothing? I keep thinking about that Adrienne Rich line about diving into the wreck. The thing itself and not the myth. I want to make something true. Something that matters. But I also want to pay my electricity bill and not have a panic attack every time my card gets declined.

The residency helps. God, it helps so much. But it's three years and then what? Back to the hustle. Back to applying for everything, hearing nothing. I'm too old for this uncertainty. Or maybe I'm exactly old enough to know it never goes away.

Nudge: "You mention being 'too old for this uncertainty' but also describe being consumed by work that matters to you. How do you sit with both of those truths?"

Response: I suppose I've always thought I'd have to choose eventually. That at some point the responsible thing would be to give up the art and get a real job, whatever that means. My father certainly thought so. But here I am at fifty-one, and I haven't chosen. I just keep doing both—worrying and working. Maybe that is the choice. Maybe the tension is the point, not something to resolve. The discomfort keeps me honest. Keeps me from getting too comfortable with my little grants and my little installations while the world burns.

Studio day. Cut seventeen panels for the base structure. Hands are sore. The plywood is heavier than I remembered, or I'm weaker. Probably both.

Got a call from the arts center about scheduling. They want to move my opening back two weeks. I said fine. It doesn't really matter.

Leftovers for dinner. Early bed.

The news today. Another boat capsized in the Mediterranean. Forty-seven people. They had names. They had families waiting for them. And I'm here in my warm studio, making art about their suffering while drinking expensive coffee.

I called Ingrid and cried on the phone for half an hour. She said the work matters. She said bearing witness matters. I want to believe her but some days it feels like decoration. Like we're all just rearranging deck chairs.

My mother called later, worried about me. She heard something in my voice this morning. I told her I was fine. I'm not fine, but what can she do? She's eighty-three. She doesn't need my existential crises.

The panels are half-finished. Tomorrow I'll keep cutting. That's all I know how to do.

Nudge: "You describe feeling like the work is 'decoration.' Has there been a time when your art felt more connected to real impact?"

Response: Yes. In 2019, I did an installation at a refugee center in Berlin. Nothing fancy—just photographs and recorded voices, stories from people who'd made the crossing. A woman named Fatima came to the opening. She'd been one of the voices. She stood in front of her own image and wept, and then she hugged me. She said no one had ever asked her to tell her story before. That it mattered that someone wanted to listen.

I think about Fatima often. Whether she'd think this new work was useful or just... European guilt made pretty.

Two days before Christmas. Studio closed for the holiday. I should be relieved but I don't know what to do with myself.

Bought presents for my mother. A scarf, some chocolate, a book about Danish gardens. Small things. Safe things. She doesn't want much anymore.

My back hurts. I've been hunched over those panels for weeks. Need to stretch more. Need to take care of this body that insists on getting older.

Sleep.

Christmas with my mother was quiet. We ate roast duck and watched old films. She fell asleep on the couch around eight and I just sat there, watching her breathe. Thinking about how many more of these we might have.

She asked about the installation. I tried to explain the concept—the rising waters, the displaced families, the way I want viewers to feel implicated. She nodded and said, "You've always cared too much." Not a criticism. Just an observation.

Maybe that's true. Maybe caring too much is the only way I know how to be in the world. The alternative—not caring—seems worse. Deadening.

The new year approaches. I don't make resolutions anymore. I just try to keep working. Keep showing up. Keep paying attention to what hurts.

Nudge: "When you say you 'keep paying attention to what hurts'—are you referring to the world's pain, your own, or both?"

Response: Both, I suppose. Though I'm not sure there's much difference anymore. The work has a way of blurring those lines. When I'm deep in research about displacement, I feel displaced. When I'm cutting panels until my hands ache, the ache feels earned. Like penance, maybe. Or communion. I don't know. The question feels too large for the last day of the year.

Got feedback from Katrine at the arts center today. She thinks the installation is "too heavy." Says audiences want hope. Says I should consider adding something uplifting at the end.

I smiled and said I'd think about it. What I wanted to say: hope is a luxury. Hope is what you get to feel when you're not the one drowning. But she controls my funding, so I nodded.

Ingrid says I should compromise. Pick my battles. She's probably right. She's usually right about these things. But every compromise feels like a small death. Another edge sanded down. Another truth made palatable.

Maybe I'm being stubborn. Maybe stubbornness is all I have left. The work will be what it is. If they don't like it, they can find someone else. Someone younger, cheaper, more optimistic.

Nudge: "You mention that 'every compromise feels like a small death,' yet you also nodded and smiled. What helps you decide which compromises to make?"

The base structure is done. All thirty-four panels, cut and sanded and ready for paint. I stood back today and looked at the skeleton of the thing—this rising wave that will eventually hold video projections, sound, the voices of people I've never met whose lives hang in the balance.

I thought about Katrine's feedback. The hope question. And I realized I was wrong to dismiss it entirely. Not because she's right that audiences need comfort, but because hope isn't the opposite of truth. Fatima in Berlin—she had hope. Impossible, stubborn hope. The families I've interviewed for this project, the ones who lost everything and started over—they have hope too. It's not naive. It's a form of resistance.

So I'm adding something. Not a happy ending, but a thread. A wall of handwritten messages from people who've rebuilt their lives after displacement. Their words, their handwriting. Small acts of continuation.

My mother would say I'm softening. Maybe I am. Maybe softening isn't the same as surrender.

The residency runs until 2027. Two more years of this stability. Two more years of being able to worry about the work instead of worrying about money. I'm grateful in a way I couldn't have articulated ten years ago. Grateful and still restless. Still wanting to make something true.

Outside, the harbor is gray. Winter light. The water rises imperceptibly, day by day. We're all just trying to stay afloat.

The department meeting today went as expected. Principal wanted us to adopt the new digital assessment platform by January. Several younger teachers were enthusiastic, but I noticed Meera sitting silently, looking overwhelmed. She is only in her second year and already struggling with the curriculum changes from last term.

After the meeting, I walked with her to the staff room and showed her how to break down the transition into smaller steps. She seemed relieved. I did not mention this to anyone else because it might embarrass her. These small moments make me feel I am contributing something worthwhile, even if no one else notices.

Amma's blood pressure was high again this morning. Cancelled my NGO session to take her to the clinic. Feeling stretched thin.

Nudge: "What's weighing on you most about today?"

Response: The children at the tutoring center were expecting me. I had promised to bring the geometry worksheets I prepared. Now another volunteer will have to scramble, and the children will wonder why I didn't come. At the same time, Amma needed me and there was no question about staying. But sitting in that clinic waiting room, I kept thinking about those children waiting too, and feeling like I was failing someone no matter what I chose.

Staff lunch today, celebrating Lakshmi ma'am's retirement after thirty-five years. Everyone contributed dishes, told stories. The principal made a speech about her dedication. I sat there eating my rice and watching her accept a shawl and a plaque, thinking about how she spent three decades in the same building, teaching the same subject, attending the same festivals. She looked content.

My brother called afterward asking when I am planning to retire. His son is getting married next year and he wants to know if I can take extended leave to help with arrangements. I said I would see what is possible. The truth is I have not thought seriously about retirement. Teaching still feels meaningful most days, and I do not know what I would do with empty hours. But my brother sounded like he was already counting on my availability, and I did not want to disappoint him by saying I prefer to keep working.

There is also the question of my pension and whether the timing would be right. I have been careful with money but there are always uncertainties. Maybe I should start considering it more seriously. The conversation left me feeling unsettled in a way I cannot quite name.

Nudge: "You mentioned not wanting to disappoint your brother. What about what you want?"
Finished grading the Class 10 exams. Most students improved from last term. Ravi, who was failing in September, scored 72%.

My colleague Anand said something today that I cannot stop thinking about. We were discussing the new reservation policy for faculty hiring, and he made a dismissive comment about how it lowers standards. I disagreed but only said "these policies have complex reasons" and changed the subject to exam schedules.

Later I felt angry at myself. I have taught students from every background for nearly three decades and I know that talent is not distributed by birth. But I did not say any of this to Anand. I worried about creating an awkward atmosphere in the department. Now I am sitting here wondering if my silence made it seem like I agreed. The children I tutor on weekends would not recognize this version of me, the one who says nothing when it matters.

Nudge: "You mentioned the children wouldn't recognize this version of you. Which version feels more like the real one?"

New year, same routines. I find comfort in this. The school reopened today and there is something settling about walking through the gates, hearing the morning assembly, smelling chalk and floor cleaner.

I brought sweets for the staff room, as I do every January. Small gestures maintain the fabric of a workplace. I noticed the new Hindi teacher, Priya, looking uncertain during break time. I invited her to sit with our group. She seemed grateful. I remember how difficult my first year was, how much it mattered when senior teachers acknowledged me. If I can make someone's path a little easier, that feels like enough.

Nudge: "What do you remember most about that first year?"

Response: I remember feeling like a fraud. I had my qualifications, my training, but standing in front of a classroom full of fourteen-year-olds who could sense every hesitation, I felt exposed. There was one day, maybe three months in, when a student asked me a question I could not answer. I panicked, gave some vague response, and spent the whole night convinced everyone knew I was not qualified to be there.

What saved me was Mrs. Subramanian, who taught physics. She never made a fuss, just started including me in her lunch conversations, asking my opinion on small matters, treating me like I belonged. I never told her how much that meant. She passed away six years ago. I suppose I keep doing for others what she did for me, hoping it matters the way it mattered to me.

My sister-in-law visited today and made a comment about how I spend too much time on "outside activities" instead of focusing on the family. She was referring to the NGO work. I explained calmly that I only go twice a month and always ensure Amma is cared for. She nodded but I could see she was not convinced.

I should not have to justify myself. I have managed this household for fifteen years while working full-time. I have never once complained about cooking for her when she visits or adjusting my schedule around her preferences. The tutoring gives me purpose beyond daily obligations. But I did not say any of this. I just served tea and changed the subject to her daughter's college applications.

Nudge: "What does that purpose feel like when you're there with the children?"

Response: It feels like my skills matter beyond grades and syllabi. These children, most of them are first-generation learners. Their parents work long hours in construction or domestic help. When I explain a concept and I see understanding appear on a face that was confused a moment before, it is the same satisfaction I felt in my first years of teaching, before it became routine.

At school, I am Padmini ma'am, the department head who files reports and attends meetings. At the tutoring center, I am just someone who knows something useful and is willing to share it. The children do not care about my title or my seniority. They only care that I showed up and that I can help them understand fractions. It is simpler and somehow more honest.

Reading the news this morning about the air quality crisis in Delhi. Schools closed, children wearing masks, hospitals filled with respiratory cases. I kept thinking about the children there who have no choice but to breathe that air, whose families cannot afford air purifiers or trips to cleaner places.

We complain about Chennai traffic and summer heat, but this is different. This is a slow catastrophe that everyone sees but no one seems able to stop. The article mentioned that the poorest neighborhoods have the worst air quality because they are near industrial zones. Of course they do. It is always the same pattern, those with the least resources bearing the heaviest burdens.

I mentioned this to a colleague during lunch and she shrugged, said "what can we do, these things are beyond us." I understand the impulse. The scale of the problem makes individual action feel pointless. But I kept thinking about it through my afternoon classes. The students I teach will inherit this world. What am I preparing them for, if not to care about things larger than their exam scores?

Nudge: "What would it look like to prepare them to care about things larger than exams?"

Response: That is the question, isn't it. I am not sure I have an answer. The syllabus is the syllabus, the exams are the exams. But sometimes I try to use real examples in my word problems. Calculating pollution levels, or ratios of resources. Small things. Maybe it plants a seed, maybe it does not. I cannot solve the crisis but I can teach the children who might.

Difficult day at the tutoring center. One of the regular students, Kavitha, did not come. I asked around and learned her family is moving back to their village because her father lost his job. She is twelve and had been making such progress in mathematics. Now she will attend the government school there, which may not have the same resources.

I keep thinking about all the Kavithas, the children whose potential depends on circumstances they did not choose. I can help a few, a handful each year, but there are so many more. The NGO coordinator asked if I could take on additional sessions. I said I would think about it, but I already know my family obligations make this difficult. Amma needs more care as she gets older, my brother's wedding preparations are starting, and I cannot ask others to carry my responsibilities so I can spend more time elsewhere. But it sits heavily, knowing there are children who need help and I am not able to give it.

Nudge: "What would it feel like to say yes to those additional sessions?"

Response: It would feel like choosing. And I have spent my whole life arranging things so I do not have to choose, so everyone gets enough of me, even if no one gets all of me. If I said yes to more sessions, I would have to say no to something else, and then I would have to explain that no, and see the disappointment, and feel like I am being selfish.

The strange thing is, I know I am not selfish. I have given and given for decades. But somehow the idea of asking for something, of prioritizing what feeds my soul over what is expected, still feels like a transgression. Kavitha's face keeps appearing in my mind. I cannot save her from moving away, but maybe I could have helped more children like her if I had been braver about asking for time that was truly mine.

Got into it with Sven about the dishes again. I ended up just doing them because it was easier than listening to him say I was making a big deal out of nothing. Whatever. It's done.

Nudge: "Does 'easier' actually mean easier?"

Sunday dinner at Mam's. She had the whole table set up nicely, the good plates, which she only does when she wants to have a Serious Conversation. Sure enough, between the stamppot and dessert she brought up the HBO thing. She had printed out the re-enrollment deadlines and put them next to my plate. My brother Thijs just kept eating and looking at his phone.

I told her I would look at it this week. Same thing I said last month. She nodded like she believed me, which almost made it worse. The bike ride home was long and cold and I kept rehearsing what I could have said instead. Something like, I do not actually know if I want to go back. But I could see exactly how her face would change if I said that, so I didn't.

The flat was quiet when I got home. I made tea and sat in the kitchen for a while. There is this crack in the ceiling tile above the stove that I keep meaning to tell the landlord about but never do.

Nudge: "What would her face do?"

Response: That thing where she goes quiet and her mouth gets tight and she starts clearing plates even though no one is finished. I have seen it once before, when Thijs dropped out for good. I cannot be the second one.

Had a decent shift today. Twelve deliveries, no flats, tips were okay. There was this moment around six when I dropped off Thai food to this older woman in Lombok and she said, oh you are always so polite, I recognise your bicycle. That stuck with me for some reason. I do not get told things like that a lot.

On the ride back to the centre I was thinking about how the delivery thing actually suits me in some ways. You show up, you do the route, people are happy to see you because you have their food. Nobody asks you to have an opinion. You just do the thing correctly and move on. Thijs thinks it is a dead-end but he also quit his warehouse job after three weeks because the manager annoyed him. I would never do that. Even when dispatch sends me across the city for a single order, I just go.

I stopped at the Albert Heijn on Voorstraat and bought stroopwafels for the flat. Sven was actually in a good mood and we watched some Champions League highlights. Felt normal. Felt like how things should be.

Moving job today with a guy named Daan who I got paired with through the app. We shifted a whole studio flat's worth of furniture from Overvecht to De Meern. Tight stairwells, a couch that did not fit through the door the normal way. The usual.

Daan is one of those people who talks constantly while working, which is fine. He was going on about how the platform takes too big a cut and we should negotiate for better rates. He wanted me to sign some kind of group message to the company. I said yeah, maybe, and kept carrying boxes. I do not really see the point. They would just replace us. There are fifty other guys who would take the jobs tomorrow.

After we finished, the client gave us both twenty extra in cash, which was nice. I biked home through Amelisweerd because the weather was good. Made pasta, did some laundry. Texted Mam a photo of dinner because she worries I do not eat properly.

Nudge: "Would you actually want better rates though?"

Response: Obviously yeah. But signing group complaints is how you get flagged as difficult. I have seen guys lose their ratings over stuff like that. Not worth it.

Marco from downstairs knocked on our door at half ten to say the music was too loud. It was not even my music, it was Sven and Bram watching some livestream thing, but I was the one who answered the door so I was the one who apologised. I turned the volume down myself before going back to my room. Nobody asked me to and nobody thanked me for it.

I lay in bed thinking about the fact that I always seem to end up being the one who makes the peace. When Marco has a problem, he knocks. When Mam has a worry, she calls. When the flat is a mess, somehow I notice first. It is not that I mind exactly, it just does not seem to work the other way around. If I am annoyed about something I keep it to myself because bringing it up would be more hassle than it is worth.

Delivery earnings were average this week. Weather is supposed to get warmer, which means more cyclists on the road competing for the same orders. I need to figure out a better evening route.

Nudge: "What would 'working the other way around' look like?"

Response: Someone noticing that I cleaned the kitchen without me saying anything. Or Sven turning the music down before Marco even has to come up. Just people paying attention the way I pay attention. Probably asking too much.

Mam rang and said Thijs told her I have not actually looked into re-enrolling. I do not even know how he knows that. I sat there on the phone saying nothing while she asked me if I have a plan and all I could think was, why did he have to say anything.

Went out for a ride after the call to clear my head. Ended up doing three deliveries even though I was not on shift. It helped. Moving helps. You do not have to think about what to say to anyone when you are just pedalling.

Nudge: "What would you say if you let yourself?"

Response: That I stopped because the lectures made me feel stupid and I was too embarrassed to tell anyone. That's it really. Not complicated.

Flat meeting tonight because the lease renewal is coming up. We all sat around the kitchen table and Bram said he might not sign because his girlfriend wants them to get their own place. Sven immediately started talking about who could replace him, rent shares, logistics. I just sat there doing maths in my head.

What I did not say is that if Bram leaves, the dynamic changes completely. Bram is the easy one, the one who never starts arguments. Without him it is me, Sven, and whoever new, and I already know I will be the one making sure everything stays smooth. I almost said something about it but Sven was already moving on to the internet bill, so I let it go.

Nudge: "What were you about to say before you let it go?"

Response: That maybe we should talk about whether we actually all want to keep living together, not just whether the numbers work. But Sven had already moved on and bringing it back up would have felt like I was making it a bigger thing than it needed to be.

Spent most of the morning reviewing the same pull request I started Friday. Nothing wrong with it, just one of those days where your eyes slide over code without absorbing anything. Had chai with Suresh at lunch and he was talking about some new cloud migration project, and I kept thinking about whether Amma took her evening medication yesterday. I should set a reminder but Priya already handles it. I just worry.

Ananya came home from tuition looking drained. She barely touched the sambar. I wanted to ask how her mock exams went but Priya gave me that look, the one that means let her eat first. So I just sat there passing the rice. Normal Monday, I suppose.

Everyone keeps asking why I didn't take the fintech offer. Suresh brought it up again. I have a good position, my PF is solid, Amma and Appa are right here, and I don't need to justify that to anyone.

Nudge: "What exactly does Suresh say that gets under your skin?"

Response: He implies I'm too afraid to try something new.

Appa's knee specialist wants to do a second surgery. The first one was only eight months ago. I told Priya we should get another opinion, but she thinks the doctor knows best. I didn't push it but something doesn't sit right.

Nudge: "What part of the doctor's reasoning bothers you?"

Response: Maybe it's not the doctor. Maybe I'm scared of Appa going through all that pain again.

Quarterly review went fine. Same feedback as always — solid, dependable, keeps the team stable. Raghavan mentioned they might need someone to lead the new payments integration and my name came up, which is nice I guess. Nothing I haven't done before. Sometimes I wonder if that's a compliment or just inertia. Like they know I won't leave so they keep handing me things.

Vikram didn't come for Sunday puja again. Third week in a row. Priya says he's sixteen and it's normal, but she didn't grow up going to the temple every week with her thatha the way I did. It mattered. Not the prayers exactly, but sitting together, the smell of camphor, the drive back when my grandfather would explain some story from the epics. Vikram doesn't have that and I can't seem to make him want it.

Ananya at least still comes, though she spends half the time on her phone. Small mercies.

Got an email from the fintech recruiter again. They bumped the package. Priya saw the notification on my phone and asked about it, and I told her it's nothing. She said she'd support whatever I decide, which somehow made it worse. If she'd just said don't take it, I could've closed the loop.

The thing is I looked at their Glassdoor page. Two rounds of layoffs in eighteen months. Equity that might be worth something or might be worth nothing. No real healthcare plan beyond some startup wellness thing. I have a daughter whose college fees start in two years and parents whose medical bills are only going up. People act like staying put is the coward's choice but they don't have my responsibilities.

Spent the rest of the evening helping Ananya with her physics. She's good at it. Better than I was at her age. That felt right, at least.

Made Paati's rasam recipe for the first time in months. The smell hit me and I had to step away from the stove for a minute. Priya finished it without saying anything.

Nudge: "What came up when you smelled it?"

Response: Her kitchen in Mylapore. Summer holidays. Being ten years old and not worrying about anything.

Vikram announced at dinner that he doesn't want to do engineering. He wants to study film. Film. I didn't say anything for a while and Priya jumped in with something supportive, and Ananya was watching me like she was waiting for an explosion. I just asked him if he'd thought about it seriously and he said yes. That was it. Dinner went on.

But I'm sitting here now and I don't know what I feel. My father was an engineer. His father was a civil servant. There was a path. Not that anyone forced me — I genuinely loved circuits and code — but there was a shape to things. A structure. You studied something that led to something stable, and that stability let you take care of the people around you. What does film lead to? I don't even know what questions to ask.

The worst part is I can hear my own father's voice in my head, and he would have said the same things I'm trying not to say. And Vikram would resent me for it just like I sometimes resented Appa for being so rigid about my sister's choices.

Nudge: "So what are you going to say to him?"

Response: I don't know yet. I just don't want him to stop talking to me about it.

Talked to Amma about Vikram's film idea. She surprised me — said your Paati always wanted to learn Carnatic music but her father said no, and she carried that her whole life. I didn't know that about my grandmother. All those years cooking her recipes and I never knew she had a dream that got shut down.

Doesn't change the practical reality though. The film industry is brutal and unpredictable. I told Vikram I'd look into some programs with him, actual structured ones, not YouTube tutorials. If he's serious, there should be a proper path. Priya thinks I'm trying to control it and maybe she's half right, but I can't just say go for it with no plan. That's not how I'm built.

Work was uneventful. Accepted the payments integration lead role. Raghavan seemed relieved. Seventeen years and I'm still the person they hand things to when they need it done without drama.

Had my quarterly review today. My director said I "exceeded expectations" and hinted at a senior manager role opening up in Q2. I should feel relieved—this is exactly what I've been working toward for two years. And I do feel good about it. But there's this part of me that immediately started calculating: if I get promoted, how long until I'm considered for the director track? It's like the goalpost moves the second I reach it.

I called Baba tonight to tell him. He was proud, I could hear it in his voice even though he just said "Alhamdulillah, keep your head down and work hard." That's his way. I think he worries I'll get complacent. I won't.

Nudge: "What would reaching the 'final' goalpost actually look like for you?"

Response: Honestly? I don't know if there is one.
Long week. Two team members called in sick, had to cover their workload on top of mine. Hit all deadlines anyway. Didn't sleep much but the project shipped on time. Director noticed.

I found out today that Ahmad—who joined the company six months after me—is also being considered for the senior manager position. We have the same title. Same level. But he's been here less time, has less experience, and somehow we're competing for the same promotion.

I know I shouldn't compare. I know the advice is "focus on your own race." But how can I not? We sit in the same open office. I see his calendar, his meetings, how he operates. He's good at being visible. Always volunteering for the high-profile projects that get executive attention, while I'm handling the operations that actually keep this company running.

Maybe that's the game. Maybe I've been playing it wrong. I thought doing excellent work would speak for itself. But excellent work that nobody sees might as well not exist.

I couldn't focus on anything else today. Kept replaying the conversation with HR, analyzing every word. They said "both strong candidates." What does that mean? Is it 50-50? Or are they just being diplomatic?

My stomach has been in knots since morning. I hate that this affects me this much.

Nudge: "It sounds like doing excellent work and being recognized for it might require different strategies. How do you feel about that?"

Response: Frustrated. It feels unfair. But I need to adapt.

End of year push. Everyone wants everything done before the holidays. I've been in the office until 9pm every day this week. My flatmate asked if I still live here—I've barely seen him.

The promotion decision got pushed to January. "Too much going on, we'll revisit in the new year." I don't know if that's good or bad. More time to prove myself, or more time to overthink everything?

Mama called asking about my holiday plans. I told her I'm staying in Dubai—too much work, flights are expensive. She was quiet for a long time. I could tell she was disappointed. My sister's engagement party is on the 27th. I should be there. But the thought of being away while Ahmad is here, possibly making moves... I can't.

I don't even know if staying will make a difference. But leaving feels like a risk I can't take.

Nudge: "Missing your sister's engagement for work that might not even matter—how does that sit with you?"

Response: It's just one event. I'll make it up to her later.

I video-called into the engagement party. It was awkward—the connection kept dropping, everyone was loud and happy and I was sitting alone in my apartment with my laptop. Layla smiled when she saw me but I could tell she'd wanted me there in person. Mama barely looked at the camera.

My cousin Tariq asked why I couldn't "just take a few days off." I said I had responsibilities. He laughed and said "Dubai changed you."

He doesn't understand. None of them do. They have stable government jobs back home, houses their families helped them buy. I'm building from nothing in a place where your visa is tied to your employment. If I lose this job, I have 30 days to find another or leave. That's not paranoia, that's reality.

But sitting here tonight, in the quiet after the call ended, I wonder if I'm using that reality as an excuse to avoid other fears.

New year. Everyone's posting about fresh starts and intentions. I don't know what mine are yet. Get the promotion, obviously. But what else?

I went for a walk along the Marina last night at midnight. All these people celebrating, fireworks going off over the water. I stood there alone and felt... nothing? Not sad, not happy. Just watching. Like I was outside of my own life looking in.

I keep thinking about what Tariq said. "Dubai changed you." Maybe it did. Or maybe this is just who I become when I'm in survival mode. Head down, eyes on the goal, don't look around too much because the periphery is full of things that will slow you down.

Is that bad? Or is that just what it takes?

Nudge: "When you say 'survival mode'—what are you surviving?"

Response: The uncertainty. The feeling that nothing is really mine.

Had a one-on-one with my director today. She was more candid than usual. Said she appreciates my "operational excellence" but that senior leadership roles require "strategic visibility." She suggested I present at the next all-hands meeting.

Part of me bristled at the feedback. I've been doing critical work, keeping the machine running while others polish their personal brands. But another part of me heard what she was actually saying: the game has rules, and I've been refusing to play by some of them.

I've always thought self-promotion was distasteful. Where I come from, you don't brag about your work—you let it speak. Boasting is seen as shallow. But I'm not where I come from anymore. I'm in a place where speaking up isn't bragging; it's survival.

I signed up for the presentation slot. It felt uncomfortable, almost wrong. But I did it.

I also booked a flight home for Mama's birthday in February. Non-refundable. I need to stop letting work consume everything. The promotion matters, but it can't be the only thing that matters.

Nudge: "What made you decide to book that non-refundable ticket?"

Response: I realized I was building a life I wouldn't want to live.

Did the all-hands presentation. Nervous beforehand but it went well. Several people came up after to compliment the insights. Ahmad was one of them—said he learned things about the operations he didn't know.

Strange to think of him as just a colleague now, not a threat. The competition hasn't changed. But my relationship to it has, slightly.

I got the promotion.

The email came this afternoon. Senior Operations Manager, effective February 1st. A 15% salary increase and a team of twelve direct reports instead of five.

I sat in the conference room alone for ten minutes after reading it, just letting it settle. Two years of work. All those late nights, the stress, the sacrifices. It paid off.

I called Baba first. He said "This is good. Keep going." Classic Baba. Then I called Mama and she cried, which made me tear up too even though I tried not to. "I'm so proud of you, habibi. When are you coming home?" I told her February, for her birthday, and she made me promise three times.

Tonight I'm going to dinner with colleagues to celebrate. I picked a nice place, not the most expensive because I'm still me, but nice enough to mark the occasion.

Sitting here now, I'm thinking about what I wrote a few weeks ago—about not knowing what the final goalpost looks like, about whether there even is one. I still don't know the answer. Maybe there isn't one. Maybe that's okay.

What I do know is that I can't let the pursuit of the next thing erase the moment when I actually get something I worked for. Tonight, I'm going to let myself feel this. Tomorrow the new worries will start: proving myself in the senior role, saving for the apartment, thinking about what's next. But tonight, just tonight, I made it.

Four hundred bucks today, which is solid, but man my knee is screaming. Had this moment around 3pm where I was sitting in my car outside some guy's apartment waiting for him to come down and I just started laughing because I remembered this time in 2019 when I was rappelling into a canyon fire in New Mexico and thinking "this is it, this is what life should feel like." Now I'm waiting for some dude to get his Thai food.

It's not bad though. Like I genuinely don't hate this. The freedom part is real—nobody tells me when to start, when to stop, I can take a Tuesday off if I want to drive up to the mountains and just sit. Rachel thinks I'm avoiding something. Maybe I am. But four hundred bucks is four hundred bucks and the drone business hit $800 in sales this month. That's momentum.

Nudge: "What does Rachel think you're avoiding exactly?"

Response: She thinks I'm avoiding commitment. Like capital-C commitment—a real job with benefits, a retirement plan, maybe a ring. She's not wrong that I'm not chasing those things, but I don't think I'm avoiding them either. I just don't see them as the only path. The drone thing could actually become something real. I sold a Phantom 4 Pro yesterday to a real estate photographer who found me on Facebook Marketplace. That's a client, not just a sale.

Rachel's brother came over for dinner last night and the whole thing turned into this intervention I didn't ask for. He's a project manager at Lockheed, makes probably three times what I do, and he kept asking "but what's the long-term plan here?" like I'm some kind of lost teenager. I'm 38 years old. I've fought wildfires. I've pulled people out of avalanches. I've built a side business from nothing while working 50-hour weeks in a car. But sure, Kevin, tell me about your 401k match.

The thing that pissed me off the most is that Rachel didn't say anything. She just sat there nodding at some of his points. We talked after he left and she said she "understands both sides" which is code for "I agree with him but don't want to fight." I get that she wants stability. I do. But I'm not going to take some cubicle job just so Kevin can stop worrying about me at Thanksgiving.

I know I sound defensive. I am defensive. But there's a difference between not having a plan and having a plan that other people don't recognize as one. The drone business did $800 this month. If I can hit $2000/month by December, that's rent covered. That's not nothing. That's a runway.

Nudge: "You mentioned runway—but runway to what?"

Response: To not having to drive for DoorDash anymore. To selling drones full-time, maybe training people to fly them, partnering with the real estate guy to do aerial shoots. There's a path here. It's just not a straight line with a pension at the end. It's more like—I build something piece by piece until one day I look around and I'm not hustling anymore, I'm running something.

Still thinking about last night. Couldn't sleep. Kevin's voice in my head: "But what if the drone thing doesn't work out?" Like failure is this thing that only happens to people who try unconventional stuff. His job is so safe it's practically embalmed and he acts like that makes him wise.

I texted my old crew chief from the fire days, Dario. He's in Tucson now doing hazmat consulting. He said "bro you gotta stop explaining yourself to people who already decided you're wrong." He's right. But it's hard when one of those people sleeps next to you.

Worked 11 hours. Three apps running simultaneously. Knee is bad. Rachel and I haven't really talked since the Kevin thing and the apartment feels weird.

Nudge: "Weird how?"

She finally said it. "I don't know if this is working for me." Not the relationship—the whole thing. The uncertainty. The not knowing if I'll make $300 or $500 in a given week. The drone boxes stacked in the spare room. The fact that I'm 38 and we're still splitting rent instead of building toward something "together."

I told her I AM building toward something. She said "but it's YOUR something. Where am I in it?" And I didn't have a good answer for that. Because honestly I haven't thought about it that way. I've been so focused on proving that my way works that I forgot to check if she wanted to be on the ride.

But also—and I know this sounds cold—is it my job to make her feel secure? She knew who I was when we started dating. I was doing ski patrol then, seasonal work, no guaranteed paycheck. She thought it was exciting. Now it's a problem? People want the story of the adventurous guy but not the actual life that comes with it.

I don't know. Maybe I'm the one who's wrong here. But it doesn't feel wrong. It feels like I'm being asked to become someone else.

Nudge: "Who would you become if you gave her what she's asking for?"

Response: I don't even know what she's asking for. A different job? A proposal? A five-year plan with bullet points? She says "stability" but that means different things. My parents had stable jobs and they were miserable for 30 years. I'd rather be broke and building something than comfortable and dead inside.

Third entry today, I know. Can't stop spinning on this.

Rachel went to her sister's for the night. Said she needed space to think. I said okay and then I sat in the apartment alone eating leftover pad thai and feeling like the walls were closing in. Which is ironic because I literally fought claustrophobia for years—you can't do wildland work if you panic in tight spaces—but emotional claustrophobia is apparently different.

I pulled up my old photos from the fire days. There's one from a job in Oregon, 2017, where we're all standing in front of this blackened hillside at dawn after a 14-hour shift. Everyone's covered in ash and grinning. We'd just saved a subdivision. I remember feeling like I mattered, like the work mattered, like I was exactly where I was supposed to be.

I don't feel that now. The drone stuff is cool when it's working but it doesn't hit the same. And driving—driving is just time traded for money, nothing more. I keep telling myself it's temporary, it's a bridge, but bridges are supposed to lead somewhere and I'm not sure mine does anymore.

Maybe Rachel is right to question it. I just wish she questioned WITH me instead of AT me.

Rachel came back this morning. We talked for two hours. She's not leaving, but she's also not okay with things continuing exactly as they are. We agreed to try couples counseling, which I never thought I'd say yes to, but here we are.

She made one point that stuck: she said she doesn't want me to change who I am, she wants to feel like she's part of the plan, not just watching from the sidelines. That's fair. I've been treating the drone business like my thing and the relationship like our thing, but they're not separate. If I succeed, she's there. If I fail, she's there. She deserves to know the stakes.

Nudge: "How'd it feel saying yes to that?"

Response: Honestly? Terrifying. Counseling feels like admitting something is broken. But maybe that's the old me talking—the guy who thinks asking for help is weakness. Dario told me once that the smartest firefighters are the ones who call for backup early. Maybe relationships work the same way. I'd rather get help now than watch the whole thing burn down because I was too proud to call it in.

First counseling session was yesterday. Weird to have a stranger summarize your relationship in 50 minutes. She said we have "different risk tolerances" and "misaligned timelines." Which, yeah, that tracks. But she also said something that hit different: she said I might be using constant motion as a way to avoid examining whether I'm actually happy.

That's been rattling around in my head ever since. Because the thing is—I'm not sure I know what happiness looks like when it's not tied to a goal. Like, I was happy fighting fires because I was good at it and it was hard and people respected it. I was happy when the drone business made its first sale because it proved the concept worked. But am I happy just... existing? On a Tuesday afternoon with nothing to prove?

Rachel asked me later what I'd do if I couldn't work. Not retirement—like, if I got injured again, worse than the knee, and couldn't drive, couldn't rebuild drones, couldn't hustle. What would be left? I said "you" but even as I said it I wasn't sure I believed it. Because I don't know who I am when I'm not chasing something.

That's a problem, probably. The counselor would say it's a problem. Rachel would say it's a problem. But part of me wonders if it's just... how I'm built. Some people are wired to sit still. I'm wired to move. The question is whether that's a feature or a bug, and whether the answer even matters if it means losing someone I love.

Nudge: "What would it look like to be still and not feel like you're dying?"

Response: I genuinely don't know. My dad retired at 62 and was dead by 65—not physically, but like, the light went out. He used to build furniture in the garage and one day he just stopped. Said there was no point. That terrifies me more than anything Rachel's brother ever said about 401ks. So maybe stillness isn't the goal. Maybe it's about finding the right kind of motion—something that includes her, something that matters, something that isn't just running from the thing I'm afraid of.

Eleven hours behind the wheel today. My knees ache in a way that reminds me I am not the young man who once walked to the mosque before dawn without thinking twice. But the tips were good, and I have now saved enough for the lamb. Hassan does not know — he would insist on paying himself, and that is precisely why I cannot tell him.
