My father slaughtered the lamb for my aqiqah with his own hands. I watched him do the same for my sons. Here in Michigan there is a halal butcher who will prepare everything properly. Different hands, same intention. I tell myself this is enough. Some nights I believe it.
Picked up a young couple from the airport. They asked if I minded them eating in the car. I said no. They left crumbs everywhere. I vacuumed at the gas station before my next ride. This is the job.

Layla asked me today why I still drive when my back gives me trouble. She said it gently, the way she says everything, but I heard what she meant: why don't you rest? Why don't you let Hassan take care of things?

I did not know how to explain that resting is not rest for me. My father worked until the week before he died. His father the same. A man provides. A man contributes. I know Layla was raised here and thinks differently about these things, and I love her for making space in her home for me, but I cannot sit in that house eating food I did not earn while my grandchildren grow up thinking their grandfather is just an old man who watches television.

She meant well. I know this. But I could not look at her when I answered, because if I had, she would have seen too much. I said something about needing the exercise, about enjoying the passengers. Lies dressed up as half-truths. The real answer lives in my chest where I cannot reach it with words.

Tonight I drove until midnight. Every fare felt like proof that I am still useful.

Nudge: "You want them to see you're useful, but you're hiding the proof."

Response: Because if I show them the money, they will say I am too old to earn it. The secret is the only way the work stays mine.

Same-day entry, different weight. Woke up and my first thought was about the conversation yesterday. Then Fajr, then coffee, then Hassan asked if I wanted to come to his office for lunch because they are having some kind of celebration for a coworker. I said yes because he rarely asks.

His office is all glass and computers and young people in clothes I do not understand. They were kind to me. Someone asked what I did before driving and I said accounting and they seemed surprised, then interested. One young woman asked about Jordan and I talked about Amman in a way I had not talked about it in months. She listened.

On the drive home Hassan said "You should come more often, Baba." I am still thinking about why that made my eyes sting.

Three rides cancelled after they saw my name. Fourth passenger asked where I was "really" from. I answered. He said nothing for the rest of the trip. No tip. I am too tired to feel anything about this tonight. Tomorrow I will feel it.

Nudge: "Is the tiredness about the hours, or about something else?"

Response: Both. Always both. Not something I have words for right now.

My brother called from Amman to say I should come back. "What are you doing there, driving strangers around? You had a career. You had respect." He does not understand that respect here is different, that I am building something he cannot see from where he sits.

He thinks I left everything behind. He does not know that I brought everything with me — the prayers, the food, the way I greet the older men at the mosque. I did not abandon our customs. I carry them. The car is just a car. What I do between the rides, who I am when no one is watching, that is what matters.

I told him I was happy. He did not believe me. That is his problem, not mine.

The aqiqah was yesterday. I did not write then because I wanted to hold the day without examining it.

The lamb was perfect. The butcher did everything correctly and I stood with Hassan as we distributed the meat — a third to the family, a third to friends, a third to those in need. My grandson slept through most of it, which is as it should be. He will not remember this day. But I will remember his face, and Layla holding him, and the way Hassan looked at me when he realized I had paid for everything.

He tried to argue. I said: "This is my right as his grandfather. You will have your turn when your son has a son." He went quiet. Later, when the guests had left, he came to me in the kitchen and said only "Shukran, Baba." Thank you. Nothing more. It was enough.

My father would have been proud. Not of me — he did not give praise easily — but of the continuation. That we are still here, still doing what was done for us, still passing it forward. The country changed. The language around us changed. But the lamb was shared according to the old ways. That matters. That is the thread I refuse to let break.

Nudge: "What else feels like that kind of thread for you?"

Response: Friday prayers. The recipes my mother taught Layla. Teaching my grandson the Arabic words for grandfather and bread and home before he learns them in English. Small things. But small things are how the rope stays woven.

Second entry today. Drove three hours after the evening prayers. Quiet roads. One passenger, an older woman going to the hospital to visit her husband. We did not speak much but she thanked me twice when I helped her with the door. Some shifts feel like service. Tonight was one.

Nudge: "What made you decide to book that non-refundable ticket?"

Response: I realized I was building a life I wouldn't want to live.

Did the all-hands presentation. Nervous beforehand but it went well. Several people came up after to compliment the insights. Ahmad was one of them—said he learned things about the operations he didn't know.

Strange to think of him as just a colleague now, not a threat. The competition hasn't changed. But my relationship to it has, slightly.

I got the promotion.

The email came this afternoon. Senior Operations Manager, effective February 1st. A 15% salary increase and a team of twelve direct reports instead of five.

I sat in the conference room alone for ten minutes after reading it, just letting it settle. Two years of work. All those late nights, the stress, the sacrifices. It paid off.

I called Baba first. He said "This is good. Keep going." Classic Baba. Then I called Mama and she cried, which made me tear up too even though I tried not to. "I'm so proud of you, habibi. When are you coming home?" I told her February, for her birthday, and she made me promise three times.

Tonight I'm going to dinner with colleagues to celebrate. I picked a nice place, not the most expensive because I'm still me, but nice enough to mark the occasion.

Sitting here now, I'm thinking about what I wrote a few weeks ago—about not knowing what the final goalpost looks like, about whether there even is one. I still don't know the answer. Maybe there isn't one. Maybe that's okay.

What I do know is that I can't let the pursuit of the next thing erase the moment when I actually get something I worked for. Tonight, I'm going to let myself feel this. Tomorrow the new worries will start: proving myself in the senior role, saving for the apartment, thinking about what's next. But tonight, just tonight, I made it.

Spent the whole morning at Leopold's working on sketches for the cafe mural commission. There's something about sitting in a busy space that helps ideas flow — all that ambient noise and people-watching. I ordered three cups of chai and didn't feel guilty about it. The sketches are coming together. Abstract florals with these hidden eyes peeking through petals. The cafe owner wanted "something colorful" which honestly gives me so much room to play.

I keep thinking about what Neha said last week, how she can't understand spending money on experiences when we're always behind on rent. But like... what's the point of hustling if you never actually enjoy anything? I'm not being irresponsible. I finished the commission brief. I just also let myself have a nice morning. Both things can be true.

Ma called again. The usual script. "Beta, you're 22 now. Meera aunty's daughter just got placed at Infosys. When are you going to think about your future?" I tried to explain that I AM thinking about my future, that the mural commission could lead to more work with the cafe chain, that I've been approached about teaching at an actual studio. But she doesn't hear any of that. She only hears "no salary, no benefits, no husband."

I hung up crying. Not because I doubt myself — I KNOW this is what I'm meant to do. But it's exhausting to constantly defend my choices to people who refuse to see them as valid. Papa at least stays quiet now. But Ma... she makes it sound like I'm throwing my life away for finger painting.

Rohan came over with biriyani and let me rant for an hour. He gets it — his family was the same way until he started making decent money from photography. He said something that stuck with me: "They'll come around when they see you're serious. You just have to outlast their doubt." But how long is that supposed to take? And why should I have to prove myself to people who should just... believe in me?

I painted until 3am. Angry brushstrokes that somehow turned into something beautiful. Maybe that's the only way I know how to process this.

Nudge: "That sounds really heavy — the gap between how you see your path and how your Ma sees it. What would it take for you to feel at peace with that gap, even if she never comes around?"

Response: Honestly? I don't know if I can feel at peace with it. Part of me still wants her approval even though I hate admitting that.

Teaching day. Seven kids between ages 8-12, all hyped up on whatever sugar their parents gave them before drop-off. We did texture collages — fabric scraps, sandpaper, dried leaves. One kid, Advait, made this incredible piece with torn newspaper headlines layered under tissue paper. He said it was "all the boring words adults say, trapped under clouds." Eight years old. I was floored.

Sometimes I think about what I would've been like if someone had let me make weird art at that age instead of drilling multiplication tables into my skull. Maybe I'd be further along now. Maybe not. Hard to know. Anyway the studio paid me in cash and I bought myself fancy dark chocolate on the way home because why not, the day was good.

Finished the Leopold mural. Owner loved it. He's already talking about his next location.

Nudge: "That's a big milestone — completing a commission and getting repeat interest. How did it feel standing back and looking at the finished piece?"

Response: Like I could breathe for the first time in weeks. That moment when you step back and it actually looks like what you imagined... there's nothing like it.

Ran into Vikram uncle at the market today — Papa's old colleague. He made this comment about how I should "use my artistic talents for graphic design, something practical." As if he knows anything about my career. As if I haven't already considered every possible path and deliberately chosen this one.

I know people mean well. I know they're just parroting the same fears their parents parroted to them. But I'm so tired of being treated like I haven't thought this through. I've thought about it more than any of them ever thought about their own safe little corporate jobs. I made a CHOICE. Why is that so hard to respect?

Nudge: "You've had a few encounters lately where you've had to defend your path. Do you notice this affecting how you feel about your work day-to-day, or does it stay separate?"
Three workshops back to back this weekend. Good money but I'm running on fumes. Need to protect my energy better.

Had a rare quiet day. Slept in until noon, made myself a proper breakfast instead of grabbing biscuits, then just... existed. Watered all the plants. Put on a Norah Jones record. Sat by the window with my sketchbook and didn't force anything. Some days the best thing I can do is nothing at all.

I've been thinking about what success means to me. Society has this very narrow definition — salary, title, marriage, house. But when I imagine my ideal life, it's this: a studio of my own, enough commissions to stay comfortable, mornings where I can drink chai without rushing, the freedom to say no to projects that don't excite me. That's it. That's the whole dream.

People act like wanting to enjoy your life is lazy or lacking drive. But I think it takes more courage to opt out of the rat race than to join it. Anyone can chase money. Not everyone can figure out what actually makes them happy and then structure their life around it.

The mural money came through. I'm putting half toward rent and supplies, and the other half toward that weekend trip to Goa with Rohan and Meera. Non-negotiable. We need this.

Nudge: "That vision of success — studio, comfortable enough, mornings without rushing. What would have to change for that to feel within reach rather than aspirational?"

Response: Honestly it doesn't feel that far off. A few more consistent clients, maybe. Right now I'm just focused on the Goa trip.

Back from Goa. Sand in places sand should not be. Sunburn on my shoulders. Zero regrets. We found this tiny beach shack that served the best fish curry I've ever had and we went back three nights in a row. Meera finally stopped stressing about her startup for five minutes. Rohan got some incredible sunset shots. I filled half a sketchbook with quick studies — fishing boats, palm shadows, the way light hits water.

This is what I work for. Not to accumulate stuff. Just to have these moments. To sit on a beach with people I love and think "this is exactly where I want to be." The work will always be there when I get back. But time with the people who matter, doing things that feel good — that's the whole point.

Long week. Delivered the payment gateway migration three days early and nobody said a word about it. Just got folded into the next sprint like it was expected. Meanwhile Tomás presented his dashboard prototype at the all-hands and got a round of applause. A prototype. Not even in production.

I'm not bitter about it. That's just how things work. You do the plumbing, someone else gets to show off the faucet. I knew that when I chose backend over product years ago.

Camila made tamales tonight. Sat on the balcony eating them while the girls did homework inside. The jalapeños she planted last month are actually coming up. Small green shoots pushing through the soil. That's the thing about growing something—it doesn't need anyone to clap for it. It just grows.

Monday standup, same routine. Gave my update, kept it short. Marcos asked if anyone had concerns about the new API rate-limiting approach and I almost said something. Almost. Because I think the threshold they picked is going to choke under real load. I ran the numbers over the weekend, actually. But Diego already endorsed the approach and Diego is the architect, so.

Funny how that works. You have the data, you have the experience, but you also have a sense of when speaking up will just make things awkward. Diego doesn't take pushback well. Everyone knows it. Nobody says it.

Went to the gym after work. Deadlifts. There's something honest about weight on a bar. Either you lift it or you don't. No politics in iron.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd spoken up?"

Response: Honestly, probably nothing dramatic. Diego would have nodded, said "good point, let's take it offline," and then nothing would change. That's the pattern. I've seen it enough times. It's not worth the energy to test it again. Better to document my concerns in the ticket comments so there's a paper trail when it breaks. That way I'm covered without making it a thing.

Performance review cycle opened today. I hate this time of year. You have to write a self-assessment that's basically a sales pitch for yourself, and I've never been good at selling. Camila says I undersell. My manager Fernando says I need to "make my impact more visible." Everyone has advice.

The thing is, I know what I've done. Seventeen production releases this year. Zero rollbacks. Mentored two junior engineers who both got promoted. Reduced our CI pipeline time by forty percent. But writing it all out in first person with action verbs and metrics feels like performing. "I spearheaded." "I drove." Those aren't words real people use.

My colleague Ana has the opposite problem—she writes these self-assessments that read like award acceptance speeches. And it works for her. She got promoted last year. I've been at the same level for four years. So maybe the system rewards the performance, not the work.

I'll write the self-assessment this weekend. I'll use the strong verbs. I'll quantify everything. It just feels like wearing a costume that doesn't fit. My papá was a carpenter in Medellín. He didn't have to write paragraphs about how well he built a cabinet. The cabinet spoke for itself.

Nudge: "You say the cabinet speaks for itself—but does it?"

Second time writing today, which I never do. But I sat down to draft the self-assessment after dinner and something shifted.

Started listing my projects and realized I've been carrying more than I thought. The payment gateway wasn't just a migration—it was six months of reverse-engineering undocumented legacy code while keeping the live system running. The mentoring wasn't casual either. I had weekly one-on-ones with Daniela and Esteban, reviewed their PRs line by line, talked them through architectural decisions. That's not a line item. That's shaping how two engineers think.

Maybe the problem isn't that I can't write about my work. Maybe it's that where I grew up, talking about yourself like that was considered feo—ugly, tasteless. You let your elders praise you. You didn't praise yourself. My mother would have given me a look if I'd walked around listing my accomplishments.

But I'm not in Medellín anymore. And the rules here are different. Not better or worse, just different. Fernando isn't going to guess what I've done. He has thirty direct reports. If I don't tell him, he genuinely won't know.

I wrote four paragraphs. Strong verbs and all. Read it back and it sounded like someone else, but maybe that's okay. Maybe learning a new professional language isn't the same as losing the old one.

Fernando scheduled a "career path conversation" for Monday. I don't know what that means. Could be good—could be the director track opening up. Could also be them telling me to lateral into some platform team nobody wants to join. I've seen both versions. People keep saying I should be hopeful but I don't want to get set up for another no.

Had the meeting with Fernando. It was... better than expected. He said the director position is opening again in Q2, and this time he's putting me forward as the internal candidate. Not a guarantee, but a real recommendation. He said my self-assessment was "the strongest you've written" and that the team's reliability metrics speak for themselves.

I thanked him. Shook his hand. Walked back to my desk and just sat there for a while.

The strange part is I expected to feel more. Elated, relieved, something big. Instead it was quiet. Like I'd been holding my breath for a long time and finally let it out, but the exhale was so slow I barely noticed. Maybe that's what it feels like when something you've wanted actually moves closer—not fireworks, just a slight loosening in the chest.

Camila was happy when I told her. She said "finally they're seeing what I've always seen." I almost corrected her—they're not seeing me, they're seeing a version of me that learned to write in their language. But I didn't say that because it would've turned a good moment into a philosophy seminar. And she deserved to just be happy for me.

Took the girls out for helado to celebrate. Lucía got three scoops. Sometimes that's enough.

Nudge: "The version they're seeing—is that still you?"

Response: I've been thinking about that since I wrote it. And honestly... yes. I think it is. It's not the whole me. It's not the me who eats tamales on the balcony or argues about fútbol with my brother-in-law. But it's not fake either. It's the part of me that learned to operate in a system that wasn't built for people like me. A Colombian who came here with an accent and a degree nobody recognized at first.

Maybe I resented it for a long time because it felt like the system was asking me to become someone else. But writing that self-assessment—I wasn't lying. I was translating. And translation isn't betrayal. My papá spoke three regional dialects. He didn't lose himself switching between them.

Submitted my director application packet today. Fernando reviewed it yesterday and said it was solid. Included the self-assessment, three peer endorsements, and a one-page proposal for how I'd restructure the backend org.

The proposal was the hardest part. I had to suggest changes to the current structure, which means implicitly criticizing how things are done now. I kept it diplomatic—framed everything as "building on existing strengths" rather than pointing out problems. Ana said I should be bolder. Maybe she's right. But I know who's on the review panel, and bold doesn't play well with everyone.

Coached volleyball practice after work. The girls are getting better at their rotations. They're learning to trust the system—each person covers their zone, and the whole court gets covered. There's a lesson there that I keep noticing but I'm not going to spell it out.

Nudge: "You keep it diplomatic—what are you protecting?"

Interview panel was today. Four people, ninety minutes. I said what I prepared. Didn't improvise, didn't overshoot. Answered the hard question about team conflict honestly—told them I default to listening first. They nodded. Whether that's enough, I'll know in two weeks. Going to bed early. Camila left soup on the stove.

Priya got her campus placement offer today. It is a decent product company in Hyderabad with good benefits. I am not jealous of her, that is not it. She earned it completely. But it made me do the math I keep avoiding.

I have been at this startup for three years and the increments have been modest. The work is the most fulfilling thing I have done but I also know Amma's knees are getting worse and the consultation she needs is not cheap. Priya's new salary helps but she will have her own rent and expenses. I keep circling back to the same question without finishing the thought.

Nudge: "What's the question you keep not finishing?"

Had coffee with Nikhil today. He moved to a fintech company six months ago, says the work is mostly CRUD but the pay let him put his parents in a nicer flat. He was not bragging. He looked tired actually. I kept nodding and thinking about Amma's knee and the 2G kids in Hubli at the same time.

Nudge: "Can you have both or is that the problem?"

Response: I think the honest answer is I don't know yet and that scares me more than either option. If I leave for more money I know I'll resent sitting in standups about payment conversion funnels. But if Amma needs that surgery and I am still making idealistic-startup money, then what was all of it for? I keep framing it like a binary but maybe the part I can't say out loud is that I'm afraid whichever I pick I'll feel like I failed someone.

Came back to write more because I could not sleep after that last entry. Nikhil also mentioned that his old manager from the gaming company reached out to me apparently. Said they are looking for someone to lead backend on an accessibility project, adaptive interfaces for visually impaired users. The pay would be significantly better than what I make now and the work would not feel empty the way pure fintech might.

But here is the thing. If I leave the startup, the offline-sync project does not have a clear owner. Karthik could take it over eventually but he joined four months ago and the codebase is, frankly, held together with duct tape and my institutional memory. The Hubli pilot is supposed to expand to three more districts by January. If that rollout stumbles because of a handoff gap, actual kids lose access.

I know I am not irreplaceable in some cosmic sense. People leave jobs and the world continues. But the timing feels like it matters. Priya's placement means I could take a risk on staying. Or it means I finally have the breathing room to go somewhere that pays properly. Same fact, two completely different stories depending on which direction I face. Lying here staring at the ceiling fan trying to figure out which story I actually believe.

Amma asked me at breakfast why I have been quiet lately. I said work stress, which is not a lie but is not the whole thing either. She said she hopes I am not overworking myself for her sake. The way she said it made me think she knows more than she lets on. That made my chest tight for the rest of the morning.

Nudge: "What do you think she actually knows?"

The Saturday bootcamp kids presented their projects today. One of them, Farah, built a bus-route finder for her neighborhood because her grandmother takes the wrong bus sometimes. It is buggy and the UI is rough but she demoed it to the group with so much confidence. I almost lost it in front of twelve teenagers.

Nudge: "What got to you about Farah's project?"

Response: She reminded me of why I started this in the first place. She was not building it for marks or a portfolio. Spent all morning fixing the matching algorithm instead of calling back the three tutors who said they might quit. Keep choosing the code over the people. Mom called twice, didn't pick up. The platform won't matter if nobody's left to teach on it.

Thinking about yesterday. I tell myself I built this thing to help kids but half the time I'm just hunched over my laptop ignoring everyone. Soyeon asked if I was okay and I said yeah, fine. I wasn't fine. I was annoyed she interrupted me.

Nudge: "Who are you building this for right now?"

One of the students, Minjun, sent me a message through the app. Just a short thing, maybe three lines. Said his math grade went from a D to a B minus and that his tutor Hyunwoo explains things in a way that doesn't make him feel stupid. I read it on the subway and my eyes got hot. I looked up at the ceiling so nobody would see.

This is why I do it. Not the pitch decks, not the investor meetings I keep fumbling. This kid in Incheon who doesn't feel stupid anymore. I saved the screenshot. I'm going to look at it on the bad days.

Nudge: "What do the bad days look like?"

Response: Days where nobody replies to my messages. Where I eat convenience store kimbap for dinner again and wonder if I should've just stayed in school like everyone else. Where I check our user numbers and they haven't moved.

Same day, later. Had dinner with Dad. He drove all the way from Incheon, which means he had something to say. We went to the jjigae place near Hongdae station, the one we used to go to when I was still enrolled and he'd visit on weekends.

He didn't bring up school right away. Asked about the platform, how many students we have now. I told him forty-seven active pairs. He nodded, did the math in his head the way he always does. "That's forty-seven kids whose parents can't afford hagwon," he said. Then he got quiet for a while.

Eventually he said your mother worries. I said I know. He said she doesn't understand why you couldn't do both, school and this. I told him it's not that kind of thing, you can't half-build something like this. He looked at his soup and said I know, I'm just telling you what she says.

We walked to the subway together after. He didn't bring up re-enrolling. That's new. At the turnstile he put his hand on my shoulder for a second, didn't say anything, and went through. I stood there like an idiot watching him go down the escalator. I think that was him saying he gets it. Or at least that he's trying to.

My old classmate Eunji posted about getting into Samsung's graduate program. Good for her. Everyone in the group chat congratulating her. I didn't say anything. I'm not jealous, I just don't have anything to say about it. My path looks nothing like that and I'm fine with it.

Hyunwoo, the tutor Minjun likes so much, told me he's graduating next month and can't keep volunteering. He was apologetic about it, kept saying he'd try to find a replacement. I told him it's fine, don't worry about it, we'll figure it out. We'll figure it out is what I say when I have no idea what to do.

I spent the evening going through the waitlist of potential tutors. There are eleven people who signed up in the last month. I messaged all of them. Three bounced, two said they're too busy now, one asked if we pay. Five didn't reply. So that's great.

The thing is I could pay tutors. I have enough from the freelance work to cover maybe two or three stipends. But then I'd have to charge the families or find a sponsor and both of those options make me feel like I'm turning this into something it's not supposed to be. I started this because the whole point was that it's free. That nobody has to qualify or prove they deserve help. But I can't keep bleeding money either. Soyeon says I'm being stubborn about it. She might be right but I don't want to hear it from her right now.

I keep thinking about Minjun. Who's going to explain things to him in a way that doesn't make him feel stupid.

Nudge: "What would you tell Minjun if he asked why his tutor left?"

Response: That people move on and it's not his fault. That I'd find someone just as good. I'd be lying about the second part but he doesn't need to carry that. He's fourteen.

Haven't slept properly in three days. Freelance deadline tomorrow, two tutor spots still empty, Mom sent a long KakaoTalk message about my cousin's wedding that I know is really about me being twenty-two and having nothing stable. I want to reply to her but I don't know how to explain any of this without starting a fight.

Nudge: "What part of it would start the fight?"

Response: The part where I say I don't regret leaving school. She hears that as me saying her life as a teacher wasn't enough. That's not what I mean but it always lands that way.

Found a replacement tutor for Minjun. Engineering student at Yonsei, seems solid. Stayed up until 2am onboarding her. Soyeon brought me coffee this morning without me asking. I almost cried over a cup of coffee. I think I need to be better at letting people help me.

Twelve-hour shift and my feet are just wrecked. But honestly a decent day. One of my kids on the ward, Milo, he's seven and has been in and out for weeks with a recurring infection. Today he finally got the all-clear for discharge. His mom was crying and I was trying not to cry and he just kept asking if he could keep the stickers I'd been putting on his IV pole every morning.

I told him yes obviously. Grabbed a handful of extras from the supply closet and stuffed them in a ziplock for him. His mom hugged me on the way out and said I was the only nurse who remembered he was scared of the blood pressure cuff and always warned him first. I do remember that stuff. It matters.

Got home and Mom had made mac and cheese from a box, which she was very proud of. Her hip is doing better, she managed the kitchen without the walker. We ate on the couch watching Chopped reruns. I didn't have the energy to make anything real. But sitting there with the mac and cheese and Mom laughing at the contestants, I just felt like okay. This is fine. This is actually good.

Nudge: "Is that what good usually looks like?"

Lisa from work asked me again about the charge nurse thing. Said the manager is "disappointed" I turned it down and that I'm "wasting my potential." I told her I appreciate the concern but I'm good where I am. She gave me that look, the one where people think you're being lazy or afraid when really you just know what works for you.

I'm not afraid of more responsibility. I covered the entire unit by myself that time two nurses called out during the flu surge. I can handle it. I just don't want to lose my Saturdays and trade my shift for nights. Mom needs someone home in the evenings and I need at least a couple days a month where I do absolutely nothing useful. That's not a character flaw.

Nudge: "Does Lisa's opinion actually get to you or is it just annoying?"

Response: It gets to me more than I want it to. She's not wrong that I could do the job. I just don't want it right now. But hearing "wasting potential" sticks in my head longer than it should.

Mom had a rough night, woke up at 3am with hip pain and I ended up sitting with her for two hours doing ice packs and finding the right pillow arrangement. Then went straight to work at 6:30. Running on maybe three hours of sleep.

The thing that's eating at me is that at one point she was apologizing for waking me up and I snapped. Not badly, just a quick "Mom it's fine, please stop saying sorry." But I could hear the edge in my voice and she went quiet. She didn't deserve that. She didn't ask for any of this. I just — I was so tired and I had work in a few hours and the frustration came out sideways.

Day off. Slept in until 10 which felt almost criminal. Mom was already up, puttering around with her walker, watching the morning news at a volume that could wake the neighborhood. Made us both a proper breakfast — scrambled eggs with chives from the little herb pot on the windowsill, sourdough toast, fresh orange juice. She said it was better than the diner we used to go to on Sundays when I was little. I don't think that's true but it was sweet.

Spent the afternoon doing laundry and reorganizing the hall closet, which has been bothering me for weeks. Mom sat in her chair and directed me on where everything should go, like she was running a construction site. It was actually kind of fun. Found a box of old photos from when my brother Kevin and I were small. Mom got teary looking at them but in a happy way.

Kevin called in the evening. He lives in Denver now. Asked how Mom was doing and I gave him the update. He said he's sorry he can't help more and that he'll try to visit in August. I said that would be great, and I meant it, but I also know "try" usually means it won't happen. I'm not angry about it. He has his own life, two kids, a wife with a demanding job. It just is what it is. I'm the one who's here and that's the arrangement we've settled into.

Nudge: "When Kevin says 'try to visit' — what do you actually expect?"

Picked up an extra shift for Dani because her kid had a fever. Sixteen hours on my feet. I can barely think straight right now. Got home and Mom had waited up for me even though it was almost midnight. She'd left a plate of pasta in the microwave with a little note that said "eat something, mija."

I feel bad because my Saturday is supposed to be this weekend and I don't think I can do it. I rescheduled Mom's appointment to Monday and I'm keeping my Saturday. I made the decision and I feel weird about it. I don't know why I can't just want two things at once without feeling like one of them makes me a bad person.

Nudge: "Who told you wanting your own life makes you bad?"

Christmas holiday but the school WhatsApp group is still buzzing with admin sending schedule reminders. Ignored all of it. Spent the morning working on the creative writing syllabus instead—finally nailed down the module on personal narratives. Used that Chimamanda essay collection I've been sitting on for months.

Ma called to ask if I'd "at least consider" the biodata from that engineer in Pune. Told her I'm not doing the arranged meeting circuit right now, I have actual work to do. She did her disappointed sigh thing but didn't push. Small victory.

The thing is, people act like I'm being difficult when I say I want to figure out my own path. But today felt right. I mapped out exactly how I want this program to run, on my terms. No committee approval needed because it's extracurricular. That's the whole point—I carved out a space where nobody gets to tell me what works and what doesn't. If Principal Mehta wants to be involved later, fine, but the vision is mine.

Rohan texted asking if I wanted to join the cousins for drinks. Didn't go. Needed this time alone with my ideas. Maybe that makes me antisocial but I genuinely don't care right now.

Back to lesson prep. The regular curriculum stuff—grammar worksheets, comprehension passages. Mind-numbing but has to be done before term starts.

Had chai with Ananya in the evening. She's dealing with wedding planning stress (March date confirmed) and wanted to vent about caterers. Listened mostly. She asked about the writing program and seemed genuinely interested, which was nice. Said she wished she had something like that when she was in school.

New year. Ma insisted on temple visit in the morning, then the extended family lunch happened. Usual scene—aunties asking about marriage, uncles making jokes about my "career phase." Nodded through most of it.

Uncle Vijay pulled me aside to say he knows someone at a CBSE school in Delhi with "excellent growth prospects" and would I be interested. Government affiliated, good pension, stable. Everything I specifically walked away from. Told him I'm happy where I am. He looked at me like I'd said something strange.

What bothers me is that I couldn't fully explain why. In the moment I just wanted to get away from the conversation. But it's not just about being stubborn. I chose this path. The after-school program, the flexibility to try new things in class, the fact that I can actually experiment—that's worth something. Worth more than a pension honestly.

But sitting here now, I wonder if I should have pushed back harder instead of just... retreating. Like, made him understand instead of just escaping the conversation. I sort of feel like I let myself down by not articulating it better.

Nudge: "What would you have wanted to say to him?"

Term starts Monday. Spent the day at school setting up classroom—put up the new bulletin board with student work from last term, rearranged desks into that U-shape configuration I like for discussions. The janitor looked at me weird but helped anyway.

Thinking about the first week back. Class 9 will be fine, they're used to my style by now. Class 7 might be tricky—new batch, they came from a very traditional English teacher before me, lots of rote learning. Going to have to ease them into actual discussion.

Also the creative writing program. Need to finalize the registration process. Was thinking maybe just a simple Google form, keep it informal. But then if it gets popular I might need to actually track attendance and all that. Ugh, the admin side of things. Part of me wants to just keep it casual and let kids show up when they want, but that might not fly with the school if they ever want to formalize it.

Ordered biryani for dinner. Treating myself since the real work starts again soon. Ma called twice, didn't pick up. Will call her tomorrow when I have the energy.

Nudge: "What's the worst that happens if you keep it casual?"

Response: Probably nothing honestly. Mehta doesn't pay attention to extracurriculars unless parents complain. I should just stop overthinking.

First day back was fine. The Class 7 kids are shy but one girl—Meera—actually raised her hand without being called on. Asked if we'd be reading any poetry this term. Told her yes, we have Kamala Das on the syllabus. She looked excited. Those moments make it worth it.

But then the staff meeting happened. Principal Mehta going on about board exam prep and standardized assessments and how we need to "align" better with the other schools in the trust. Forty-five minutes of jargon. I could feel my eyes glazing over.

Here's what I keep circling back to: I know I'm a good teacher. The kids respond, they engage, they actually want to be in class. But in these meetings I feel like a fraud because I'm not doing what everyone else is doing. They're all talking about test scores and I'm thinking about how to get the kids to care about what they're reading.

And the thing is, I believe I'm right. I believe my approach matters. But when you're sitting in a room full of people nodding along to metrics and "learning outcomes," it's hard not to feel like maybe you're the one who's missing something.

I guess I'm just tired. It's only day one.

Nudge: "Do you actually think you're missing something, or does the room just make you feel that way?"

Response: The room definitely. When I'm with my students I know exactly what I'm doing. Meetings just bring out this performative anxiety or something.
Good teaching day. The Class 9 debate on whether social media is ruining language went better than expected—even the quiet ones participated. This is why I do this.

Furious right now. Just got off the phone with Ma and I need to write this down before I explode.

She forwarded me a LinkedIn profile. An ENGINEER from her friend's son's network who "seems compatible" based on absolutely nothing. When I told her I wasn't interested, she started crying. Actual tears. Said she's worried I'm "throwing my life away" by being so "particular about everything."

Particular. Like wanting to choose my own life partner is some unreasonable demand. Like deciding to leave a government job to do work that actually means something is being difficult.

I told her I'd call her back when I was calmer. She said "don't bother, you never listen anyway." So that's where we are now.

The thing that really gets me is that she knows. She knows I'm happy with my work. She's seen me come home excited about a lesson that went well. She visited the school during last year's annual day and saw the kids perform the scenes we'd been working on for months. She hugged me after and said she was proud.

But somehow that doesn't count? Somehow I'm still failing at life because I'm 29 and single and not in a government job with a pension?

I don't know how to make her understand that I need to do things my own way. I've tried explaining it. Maybe I need to just accept that she'll never get it.

Nudge: "Is that actually what you want though—her just not getting it forever?"

Response: No, obviously not. But what's the alternative? I can't change who I am to make her comfortable.

Creative writing program had its first session today. Seven kids showed up, which is more than I expected honestly. Mix of classes 8-10. We did a freewriting exercise and then shared—nobody had to but four of them volunteered.

One boy, Arjun, wrote about his grandmother's hands. Just this vivid little piece about how she makes rotis and the way she hums while she works. Nothing fancy but so specific and real. I told him it was beautiful and he got embarrassed but also clearly pleased.

This is the thing I built. Mine. Nobody assigned it to me or approved a budget or gave me a template to follow. I figured out what I wanted to create and I made it happen. Finally something that feels fully mine in this job.
Second creative writing session done. Ten kids this time—word is spreading. Meera from Class 7 joined and already has a notebook full of poems she wanted to show me.

People keep telling me I need to set boundaries, like it's so easy. My friend Ana was saying how I should stop lending money to Diego because he never pays me back. But she doesn't understand—he's family. His girls needed new shoes for school and what was I supposed to do, just say no? He's trying his best with that construction job but the hours aren't steady.

I know I can't save everyone. I know that. But when someone in my life needs help and I have even a little bit to give, how do I look them in the eye and say sorry, I'm saving for myself? Maybe I'm the fool here but I'd rather be broke and know I showed up for people than comfortable and wondering if I did enough.

Nudge: "You mention maybe being 'the fool' here—what would it look like if you weren't?"

Response: Honestly? I don't know if I'd recognize myself. Like, the version of me who doesn't pick up the phone when Diego calls, who doesn't drive 40 minutes to help tia Elena with her groceries... that person feels like a stranger. Sometimes I wonder if I help because I want to or because I'm scared of who I'd be if I stopped. My mom was the same way. She gave and gave until there was nothing left. I don't want to end up like that but I also don't know any other way to be.
Twelve hours of driving today. Picked up tia Elena's medication on my break because the pharmacy was on my route anyway. Just want to sleep.

Diego paid me back today, which surprised me. He said his girls wanted to give me their piggy bank money for helping them. I told them to keep it but I cried in the car after.

Nudge: "What was it about that moment that moved you?"

Response: I don't know, it was just sweet. Kids being kids. Anyway, it was a good day—better than most this week.
Made tamales with tia Elena for the holidays. She taught me her mother's recipe, the one with the olives. Spent the whole afternoon together, just us and the masa.

My brother called from Bogota—he's struggling with rent again. I already sent what I could last month but I'll figure something out. He's my little brother; that's just what you do.

Nudge: "When you say you'll 'figure something out'—what does that usually mean for you?"

Response: It means I pick up extra shifts, maybe skip the new tires for another month. It's fine, I've done it before. My brother didn't ask to be born into this situation, you know?

Tia Elena fell yesterday. She's okay, just bruised, but I wasn't there. I was doing a delivery across town and didn't see her call until an hour later. I should have been there.

Nudge: "You were working—how could you have known?"

Response: I know, I know. But she lives alone. What if next time it's worse? I've been thinking about asking her to move in with me but my place is so small and she's proud, she wouldn't want to feel like a burden. I don't know.

New Year's Eve was nice, actually. Ana came over with some of the other girls from the building and we did the grape thing at midnight—you know, eating twelve grapes for twelve wishes. Tia Elena made it too, even with her hip still sore. Diego's kids were running around screaming happy new year and setting off these little firecrackers in the parking lot.

I caught myself just standing in the kitchen at one point, watching everyone eat my food and laugh, and I felt... full. Not from the pernil, though that was good too. But like, okay, this is why I do everything. These people, this noise, this mess. Ana asked me what my resolution was and I said to take better care of myself but honestly I don't even know what that means. I've never been good at that part. Taking care of other people is easy—there's always a clear next thing to do. But taking care of myself feels selfish, or maybe I just don't know how.

My cousin said something that stuck with me though. She said you can't pour from an empty cup. I've heard that before but this time it hit different. Maybe because I've been running on empty for months and it's starting to show. Snapping at people in traffic, too tired to cook some nights, just ordering delivery even though I can't afford it. I want to do better this year but I don't know where to start.

Nudge: "What's one small thing that would feel like taking care of yourself?"

Response: I keep thinking about Sunday mornings. When I was little, before my mom got sick, she used to make herself coffee and sit on the porch alone for like an hour. She called it her "moment." I never understood it then—I thought she was just being lazy. Now I get it. I don't have a porch but I could... I don't know, maybe just sit with my coffee before I start driving. Not check my phone, not worry about who needs what. Just ten minutes of quiet. It sounds so small but I honestly can't remember the last time I did nothing without feeling guilty about it.

I tried the coffee thing this morning. Five minutes in, Diego texted asking if I could watch the girls after school. I said yes before I even finished reading.

Nudge: "What would it have felt like to finish your coffee first?"

Response: Probably bad, honestly. Like I was choosing myself over them. I know that's not how it works but that's how it feels. Anyway, the girls are fun to have around. It wasn't a sacrifice, just a change of plans.

Had a meeting with Dr. Villanueva about the dissertation chapter on extractive policy in Peru. For once he actually engaged with the argument instead of just redirecting me toward his framework. He pushed back on my case selection, which was fair, but he did it by asking questions rather than just telling me to change it. That felt different. I left his office thinking clearly for the first time in weeks.

Walked to the mercado afterward and sat with a coffee going through my notes. The chapter outline is solid. The theoretical framing is mine -- not his, not borrowed from the lab's usual line. I've been circling this argument for months and it finally has shape. There's a version of this that could become a standalone article, which matters because I need publications that aren't just appendages to his projects.

I forget sometimes that I came here with a decade of professional experience. I wasn't some kid who wandered into a program. I chose this. I left a salary and a title because the work I was doing in Bogota hit a ceiling I couldn't break through without the credential. Days like today remind me the trade-off has a point.

Nudge: "That chapter outline is yours -- what usually pulls you away from it?"

Lab meeting today was three hours of performative agreement. Villanueva presented his new grant proposal and everyone nodded along like it was revelation. Half the methodology section is recycled from a paper we all know didn't replicate well. I sat there biting my tongue because the last time I raised a concern in front of the group he reframed it as me "not understanding the nuance yet." In front of the first-years. That was six months ago and I still feel it.

Marina, the other senior student, caught my eye during his presentation. She sees it too but she's smarter about it than I am -- she just stays quiet, publishes on the side, and keeps her head down. I can't do that. Something in me won't let me just sit there when the reasoning is sloppy. But speaking up costs more than staying silent, at least in this lab.

Came home and opened the Peru chapter again. The contrast is sharp. In Villanueva's lab I'm a student who should listen. In my own work I'm building something that could actually matter for how these countries manage their mining conflicts. The gap between those two versions of me is getting harder to hold.

Nudge: "What does Marina's approach cost her that yours doesn't?"

Got an email from a former colleague in Bogota asking if I'd co-author a policy brief on lithium regulation for a think tank. Good money, my name on it, and the topic overlaps with my dissertation. I said yes immediately. Then spent the rest of the afternoon wondering if I should have checked with Villanueva first. The program technically doesn't require it, but there's an unspoken expectation that outside work gets cleared through him.

The thing that bothers me is the hesitation itself. I spent eight years writing policy documents without asking anyone's permission. Now I'm second-guessing a decision I'm fully qualified to make because someone with a title might disapprove. I don't know when I started treating his opinion as a gate I have to pass through, but I notice it more and more.

Same day, later. Called my mother in Cali to tell her about the policy brief. She was happy, asked how much it paid, then reminded me that my cousin Luis just got promoted to director at the ministry. She doesn't mean it as a comparison but it lands like one. Luis is 33, has a corner office, and never left the country. I'm 38, sharing a desk in a research lab.

But then she said something that settled me: "You always needed to understand why things work, not just that they work." She's right. That's why consulting felt hollow toward the end. I was writing recommendations I didn't fully believe in because the analysis was never deep enough. The PhD is slow and humbling but at least I'm doing the actual thinking now.

Nudge: "Is the thinking deeper now, or just differently constrained?"

Response: Different constraints, sure. But at least I chose these ones.

Villanueva told me today that he wants to be listed as co-author on the Peru chapter if I submit it to a journal. His reasoning is that the "intellectual environment of the lab" contributed to the work. I pushed back. Told him the chapter uses a different framework than anything the lab has produced, that the fieldwork contacts are mine from my Bogota years, that the data collection was funded by my own assistantship hours. He got quiet in that way he does -- not angry, just recalibrating.

He said we'd "revisit it later," which means he expects me to come around. I won't. This is the one piece of work in this entire program that is entirely mine. Every argument, every source, every late night rewriting the methodology section because his suggestions didn't fit. I'm not handing that over.

I know how this looks from outside. Grad student refusing to share credit with advisor. But this isn't about generosity. If I publish this with his name on it, it becomes part of his portfolio, his grant narrative, his legacy. My contribution disappears into his. I've watched it happen to Marina three times already. That's not going to be me.

Nudge: "What happens if he doesn't let it go?"

Response: I'll figure that out when it comes. Right now the chapter is mine.

Can't focus. Keep replaying the conversation with Villanueva. The way he leaned back in his chair. The pause before he said "revisit." I know that move because I used to do it in meetings in Bogota -- the deliberate silence that says I'm not done with this. He's not going to drop it. And the problem is he doesn't need to fight me directly. He just needs to delay things. Slow down feedback on my other chapters. Forget to sign off on conference funding. The machinery of a program has a hundred small levers.

Started the policy brief for the Bogota think tank. The writing came fast, like muscle memory. Two thousand words in three hours. It reminded me of what it feels like to work without someone standing over the output, deciding if it's good enough to carry their name.

Had lunch with Marina. She told me Villanueva asked her opinion about my "collaboration style." That's how he phrases it -- not that I'm difficult, just that my style needs adjustment. She told him I'm thorough. Diplomatic of her. We both know what he's doing. At least someone in that lab sees it clearly.

Nudge: "What would it change if you and Marina pushed back together?"

Later in the evening. Finished the first draft of the policy brief. Sent it to the think tank contact. Fourteen pages, clean argument, clear recommendations. Took me less than a week of evening work. The dissertation chapter, by contrast, has been in revision for four months because every piece of feedback from Villanueva sends me in a different direction.

Thinking about what Marina said at lunch, about his use of "collaboration style." It's a framing that puts the problem on me, on how I work, instead of on the fact that he's claiming credit for something he didn't build. Language like that is how people with institutional standing rewrite the narrative. I've seen it in government -- the person with the title gets to define the terms.

I need to decide soon whether to submit the Peru chapter independently or wait for whatever "revisit" means. Waiting gives him time to build his case or create friction. Submitting means the relationship might not recover. My mother would say "no pelees batallas que no puedes ganar." But I'm not sure this is a battle I can afford to lose either. The chapter is the best thing I've written. If it goes out with his name, the work is still good. But it won't be mine anymore.

Nudge: "Which loss would sit with you longer?"
